context.content:
My First Lesson in Stepping Out of the Comfort Zone ✨

Attended the first session of my Management Skills Workshop today. Sitting in the classroom, I felt a bit nervous. After three years of focusing purely on execution and collaboration, suddenly diving into unfamiliar territory like management and strategy felt a little overwhelming.

However, when Teacher Li brought up cross-departmental communication, I took the initiative to share a case study from my experience coordinating product, operations, and marketing for a campaign. I didn't expect him to say, "You have very sharp observations"—that one sentence made me relax instantly. I realized that what I’ve accumulated over the past three years isn't just "knowing how to execute"; it’s truly valuable experience.

🌟 My biggest takeaway:
Stepping out of your comfort zone isn't about tearing yourself down; it’s about building on top of what you already have. I don’t need to become a different person; I just need to become a more well-rounded version of myself.

These next 8 weeks might be exhausting, but now I’m actually looking forward to them. If you’re also debating whether to learn something new or challenge yourself, I’d say—instead of overthinking it within your comfort zone, just take that first step. You’ll find that you have much more potential than you imagined.

#CareerGrowth #ManagementSkills #ComfortZone #LearningDiary

Chat History - with Chen Hao

---

Haha, Wang Fang just told me that you gave her a real chance to talk. 😂 Her exact words were, "Finally, someone who can actually listen to me brag." I’m dying.

But what I really wanted to say is that you were so natural today. It didn't feel forced at all, like you were trying too hard to "make a good impression on my friends." It was just... really comfortable?

And the questions you asked were actually interesting—not those awkward "So, what have you been up to lately?" conversation fillers. Chen Si even told me later that you seemed genuinely interested in that project she mentioned and asked a lot of details. She was pretty happy about it.

I was actually a little nervous, wondering if you’d find my circle of friends a bit... loud? 😅 But seeing the way you handled the conversation, it seemed like you were totally fine.

Thank you for today. Really. I’m not just being polite, I just... feel really at ease.

Goodnight! 😊

I came across this quote while on the subway, and it suddenly brought a sense of relief.

"Only by letting go of the obsession with perfection can you truly embrace life."

Work has been a bit hectic lately, but I don't feel as anxious as I used to. It’s not that there’s less to do, but rather that I’ve started to realize—not everything needs to be solved perfectly by me alone. My team is here, my friends are here, and Chen Hao is here too.

Instead of scrolling through work chats after clocking out, I read for a bit on the subway. After getting home, I cooked something simple and shared some of my recent thoughts with Chen Hao. He said I don't look as exhausted anymore.

Maybe this is what growth is all about. It’s not about becoming "stronger," but about learning to let go of the things that aren't necessary.

Keep it up tomorrow! 💪

Fangfang, I’m having a bit of a breakdown...

That meeting just now was a total disaster. Zhang Ming pointed out the flaws in my proposal right in front of everyone. I tried to defend myself, but the more I explained, the more I felt myself losing my voice. In that moment, I just sat there feeling like everyone’s eyes were on me. I hadn't even thought of those issues...

How did I miss them? These are things I should have considered beforehand.

I’m sitting in my office now, and that scene just keeps replaying in my head. Am I really not cut out for management? Everything was so smooth when I was just an executor, but now that I’m in management, I can’t seem to get anything right. I’m starting to have regrets...

Do you think I made the wrong choice? I feel so powerless right now. Work is like this, and then I have to go home and face Chen Hao. He’s been a bit cold lately—I know it’s because I’ve been too busy, but I honestly don't have any energy left to give. Even during the last video call with my parents, I was totally checked out...

I’m so exhausted. It’s not physical; my heart is just tired. I feel like I’m failing at everything.

Are you free? I want to go for a walk, or just talk. I need to hear what you think; my head is a mess right now.

Wang Fang, I have something to tell you 😅

So, I met someone recently. His name is Chen Hao, a product manager. It’s a bit embarrassing to bring up because I kept saying I wasn't in any rush, and then... well, here we are.

But the thing is, there’s this incredibly comfortable vibe when we talk? It’s not that "love at first sight" kind of rush, but more like... how do I put it? Like finally meeting someone who truly gets you. We grabbed dinner last time and talked about career growth; his perspective was completely different from mine, but while listening to him, I just kept thinking, "Oh, I never thought of it that way." There’s no pressure to convince each other of anything—we just naturally vibe with each other's ideas.

The most amazing part is that our rhythm in life seems to be totally in sync. He doesn’t find it weird that I spend my weekends at pottery classes; instead, he asks me what I’ve learned. He doesn't rush me into making decisions either; he just... thinks things through with me?

I know this sounds like I’m bragging haha, but I mainly wanted to double-check with you—this is real, right? It’s not just all in my head? Work has been going well lately and I’ve been in a great mood, so I’m a little worried that I might just be projecting all that positivity onto him.

Are you free anytime soon? I’d love to grab a bite and hear what you think. I haven't told my family yet; I wanted to talk to you first 😊

Chen Hao, are you there?

I suddenly realized something interesting while I was on the subway just now. I opened that book you recommended instead of scrolling through my work groups like I usually do. It got me thinking—this feels like something I’ve only recently become capable of doing.

Before, I’d still be dwelling on work even after clocking out; I just couldn't switch my brain off. But today was different. I just let it go, quite naturally. Maybe it’s because I’ve mostly handled that conflict from the afternoon meeting, so I don't feel so weighed down anymore.

You know, I feel like I’m actually starting to find that sense of balance. It’s not that work isn’t important, it’s just… I’m starting to trust that I can handle things, so I don’t have to keep obsessing over them in my head. It actually helps me think more clearly.

When I got home and saw you busy in the kitchen, I really felt like talking. Cooking and chatting with you makes me feel completely relaxed. It doesn’t feel like I’m escaping work; it feels like I’m actually *living*.

I wanted to check in—what’s been on your mind lately? I don’t mean work stuff, just… how do you feel about the way our life is right now?

What I’m trying to say is, thank you for being there for me during this time. I know I was pretty anxious for a while, and you were always so patient listening to me. I’m doing much better now, and I’ve started to feel like we’re not just dealing with our own problems separately, but really going through all of this together.

It feels different now.

Wang Fang, I’m still reflecting on our conversation just now.

Sitting there in the cafe, hearing you say that I’ve "become more profound" made me realize how much I’ve actually changed over the past year. But it’s not in the "sophisticated" way you might think; it’s more like… I’m just not in such a rush anymore?

I used to feel like I constantly had to prove something, to make sure everyone saw me progressing and growing. Now, it’s the opposite. I’ve started asking myself what I actually want. Not what projects I need to finish at work or what milestones I need to hit in life, but—what kind of life do I want to live? Who do I want to be with? What makes it all feel meaningful?

You know, when you said those things, my eyes really did well up. Not because I was sad, but because of that feeling of being *seen*. You saw what I was thinking and how I was changing, rather than just looking at what I was busy with on the surface. That means the world to me.

I used to spend all my time wondering if I should get married, get a promotion, or save up a certain amount of money… like I was just checking off a to-do list. But talking with Chen Hao lately, we’ve both realized that we don’t need to follow anyone else’s timeline. We just want to live an authentic life together, regardless of what others think.

Thank you for seeing me that way. Truly.

Are we still on for dance class tomorrow? I need to move around and just dance all these thoughts out! 😄

At dinner, Chen Hao asked if things have been a bit more relaxed lately. I paused for a moment, only then realizing that I really haven't been so wound up.

It’s not that the pressure has vanished, but rather that I’m slowly starting to understand—the challenges themselves are telling you exactly who you want to become.

Sometimes, listening intently is more important than rushing to provide an answer. Sometimes, admitting "I don't know" carries more power than pretending to have it all figured out.

Eat well, talk well. Let that be enough.

[Image: A corner of the dining table, two cups of warm tea]

The best kind of companionship is the kind that needs no introduction.

It wasn't until I was chatting with Li Xin today that I realized I’ve naturally started using "we" to describe things. "We both love the theater," "Our plans for the weekend are..." — the words just flowed, as if they had always been on the tip of my tongue.

There was no grand moment; it was just a casual office chat where it suddenly hit me: this might be what intimacy feels like at its most comfortable. No need for announcements, no need for proof—just naturally weaving into each other's lives.

We are learning our own individual rhythms while finding a shared beat. The process can be a little tiring, but more than anything, it feels grounding.

Lately, I’ve been reflecting on something: Is growth just a cycle of constantly asking yourself "Am I really cut out for this?" and then suddenly finding peace through a single word from a friend?

I was chatting with Wang Fang yesterday, and she mentioned she hadn't seen much of me lately. It made me realize just how long it’s been since I truly spent quality time with the people around me. Work has been taking up way too much headspace, but it was during that conversation that I started to understand—perhaps stumbling through the imperfections is progress in itself.

Being in a management role is far more complex than I imagined. It’s not just about getting the job done; it’s about navigating all the invisible things. There are moments of self-doubt, but I’m starting to feel that the very fact I’m asking these questions might actually mean something.

To those who are still willing to listen to my ramblings when I’m feeling lost: thank you. Your understanding gives me the courage to keep moving forward.

Time to recalibrate my rhythm; I can’t keep letting the busyness crowd out companionship. Drinks/coffee next week?

[Image: A cup of coffee, afternoon sunlight streaming through the window]

Stepping out of the conference room after today’s speech, I still feel a bit surreal. 😅

From the initial "Can I really do this?" to hearing that applause today, it’s been quite a journey. A huge thank you to @WangFang for being there for me throughout this time, and to @ChenHao for being such a patient listener that night. You both helped me realize that when anxiety hits, it’s not about finding the "perfect answer," but about allowing yourself to find your way through the uncertainty.

The most rewarding part wasn't the applause itself, but the fact that several department managers approached me afterward, wanting to discuss team-building frameworks together. In that moment, it finally clicked—I’m not just "pretending" to be a manager anymore; I am actually becoming one.

I’ve realized that management isn't about being perfect; it’s about being authentic. It’s about caring for your team while standing firm in your own judgment. It sounds simple, but it took me months to truly wrap my head around it. 😂

Keep pushing next week. I’d love to hear from you all—have you ever had one of those "suddenly realizing you can do it" moments at work?

I noticed that there is key information missing from the details you provided: the **"I. Background Information of the Current Event (Detail Event)"** section is blank.

It took a conversation with Wang Fang today for me to finally realize something: what I lack isn’t ability, but a systematic body of knowledge.

This realization was like a light bulb going off, suddenly illuminating the confusion I’ve felt over the past three years. I’ve always felt I was doing pretty well in Operations, but lately, I’ve hit a bit of a ceiling. Sometimes, looking at data reports just leaves me feeling lost. During the first session of the Management Workshop on conflict resolution frameworks, Teacher Li asked us, "What is your biggest obstacle in cross-departmental communication?" That’s when it hit me—I’ve been relying entirely on experience and intuition, without ever systematically thinking through the underlying logic.

That was my biggest takeaway this week. It’s not that I’m not capable; it’s that I’ve found a clear direction: I need to approach problems with a more structured mindset. Suddenly, the path ahead feels a lot brighter 😄

Also, when I shared this with friends over dinner, they were all so encouraging. I finally feel like I’m making real changes instead of just repeating the same routine every day. I’m going to keep my head down and focus on learning for the next eight weeks to see what new possibilities I can unlock.

So grateful to have such amazing friends who always provide feedback and encouragement at just the right moments 💪

# Work Log - 2023.10.17

**Time:** 15:30  
**Location:** Office, just finished a meeting

---

Today’s team meeting is over. Sitting at my desk, my heart is still racing a little.

The conflict this morning was more serious than I anticipated. Li Xin and another colleague had a disagreement over the division of labor. On the surface, it looked like a process issue, but in reality, it reflected a failure on my part as a manager to define clear boundaries. They didn't know who was responsible for what, which led to the finger-pointing. That’s on me.

I spent the entire morning gathering my thoughts and used the afternoon team meeting to restructure the workflow. I clarified the previously vague areas: who handles requirements assessment, who executes, and who provides feedback. I also announced a new mechanism—a weekly Wednesday afternoon sync to ensure there are no more communication gaps.

To be honest, I was a bit nervous. I didn't want to come across as too heavy-handed, nor did I want to seem weak. I said one thing during the meeting that I still feel a bit proud of: "This isn't anyone's fault; it's an area where I, as a manager, need to improve."

Li Xin relaxed after hearing that, and the others nodded. The atmosphere was noticeably different by the end of the meeting.

**But I need to record a few things honestly:**

1. **Did I do the right thing?** It seems so for now. However, I realize this might just be a short-term fix. The real issue is that I need to build a more systematic management framework rather than just reacting every time a problem boils over.

2. **I’m still seeking validation.** Right after the meeting, I emailed Zhang Ming to summarize the steps I took. I know what I was doing—I was looking for his approval. That’s not great. I should trust my own judgment more.

3. **On authority vs. warmth.** I’ve been thinking about how to maintain my natural style (listening and being empathetic) while still showing the necessary decisiveness. Today’s meeting made me feel that perhaps the two aren't mutually exclusive. I didn't bark orders; I clearly explained the reasoning and the process, and that seemed more effective than I imagined.

4. **The time cost.** Handling this conflict took up most of my day. I’m supposed to have dinner with Chen Hao tonight, but I still have a mountain of unread emails. I need to figure out how to handle these things more efficiently, or I’m going to burn out.

**Next Steps:**
- Observe whether the new sync meeting mechanism actually works.
- Keep the lines of communication open with Li Xin to ensure she’s okay with the decision.
- Chat with Chen Hao over the weekend; he might have some perspectives I haven't considered.

Actually, as I sit here writing this, I realize I’m not as anxious as I was before. At least today I took action instead of doing nothing. Maybe that’s what being a manager is about—not finding the perfect answer, but making a decision amidst uncertainty and then watching the results.

We'll see how it goes.

July 5, 2023, past 9:00 PM

My phone is still on, and Chen Hao’s message is still there on the screen. I must have stared at it for five minutes, my finger hovering over the reply box, before finally switching it off. Now I’m sitting in the dark, with only a sliver of light from the streetlamp outside filtering in.

July 8th and 9th.

Just those two days. My parents said they want me to come home this weekend; Dad’s blood pressure has been a bit high lately, and Mom hasn’t been sleeping well. They want to go to the hospital together for a full check-up. I’ve marked it in red on my calendar. Then Chen Hao sent me the info for that art exhibition—the one he’s been dying to see. It’s only on the 8th and 9th. He said, "How about we go together? I really want to see this with you."

I didn’t reply right away.

I know I should. I know he’s waiting for an answer. But looking at those two dates is like looking at an unsolvable equation. Choose one side, and the other is disappointed. Choose the other, and this side loses out. I can’t even say "maybe next time," because there is no next time—the exhibition is only those two days, and my parents' appointments are already booked.

I wonder why I can’t satisfy everyone at once. Why does someone always have to be let down?

When I turned off my phone, I felt a wave of self-loathing. I hate that I can’t make a decision, and I hate that I keep avoiding it. I should have told Chen Hao sooner; I should have told my parents sooner. But I didn’t. I kept stalling, thinking, "Just wait a bit longer, maybe I’ll find a way to have it both ways."

Now I know there isn't one.

Sitting here in the dark, a strange question crossed my mind: Am I not a good enough daughter? Am I not a good enough partner? I always thought I was doing okay in life—stable job, plenty of friends, a decent relationship with my family. But now I realize that I’m just "okay" at everything, and truly "devoted" to nothing.

I remember the numbers for my dad’s blood pressure, but how long has it been since I actually asked him how he’s doing? I know about my mom’s menopause symptoms, but when was the last time I went for a walk with her? Chen Hao mentioned that exhibition three times; every time I said, "Sure, sounds great," but I never once bothered to look up the dates myself.

It feels like I’ve been using "I’m busy" as a way to forgive my own absence.

It’s 9:30 PM on July 5th, and I still haven’t replied to Chen Hao. I know he’s probably waiting. I know I need to make a choice. But I’m just sitting here, unable to do it.

Maybe I’ll think of something tomorrow. Maybe I won’t.

Right now, I’m just tired. So incredibly tired.

# Work Log - August 11, 2022

**Project:** User Operations System Optimization + Personal Growth Plan
**Date:** 2022-08-11
**Status:** In Progress

---

## Morning Summary

Finished the retrospective for the August community campaign. User engagement is up 12% month-over-month; the "User Story Collection" event turned out quite well. Li Xin provided data feedback very promptly, and cross-departmental collaboration has been smooth. This afternoon, I need to sync with Zhang Ming on the strategic direction for September's activities.

## Today’s Focus - Data Analysis Learning Plan

I spent my lunch break putting this idea into a formal document. During the first session of the workshop, Mr. Li mentioned data-driven decision-making, which made me realize that this is a major blind spot for me. I can’t keep relying on gut feeling and experience every time; I need to learn to understand the logic behind the numbers.

**My Learning Toolkit:**
1. **Advanced Excel** - Pivot tables, data visualization (the most urgent need).
2. **Google Analytics Basics** - User behavior tracking (directly applicable to my work).
3. **Python for Beginners** - A bit ambitious, but I want to try automating data processing.
4. **Tableau or Power BI** - For dashboard reporting (this might have to wait a while).
5. **Basic Statistics** - Doesn't need to be too deep, but I need to understand concepts like p-values and confidence levels.

**Study Schedule:**
- Carve out 5-7 hours per week (mostly evenings and weekends).
- Start with Excel and GA; aim for basic mastery by the end of September.
- Apply what I learn directly to next month’s campaign data analysis.

## Why Now?

To be honest, it wasn't just the workshop that triggered this. I’ve been in operations for three years now. I’m good at daily execution and user communication, but every time I see the Product team using data to back their decisions, I feel like I’m missing something. If I want to move into management or pivot toward Data Operations in the future, these skills are unavoidable.

Instead of waiting to be phased out, I’d rather bridge the gap now.

## Next Steps

- Buy a few books this weekend and start grinding through Excel.
- Talk to Wang Fang to see if she’s interested in learning together (she seems to be considering a career shift too).
- In early September, re-analyze the August user data using new methods to give Zhang Ming a fresh perspective.

---

**Notes:** Feeling a bit nervous but also excited. At least I’m doing something now instead of standing still.

# Career Direction Reflection Memo

**Date: 2022.03.10**

---

## Current Situation

During a meeting recently, a point was raised: my new campaign proposal was actually just the same old routine—old wine in a new bottle. It was a bit awkward at the time, but thinking back, it’s true.

It’s been three years. From e-commerce to my current role, I know the operations workflow by heart: Topic Selection → Planning → Execution → Review. I do a good job, and people recognize that, but the problem is... I feel like I’m just repeating the same thing over and over.

**Keyword: The Comfort Zone Trap**

---

## Points of Confusion

1. **Is being "reliable" an asset or a ceiling?**
   - The team needs me as an executor, and I deliver consistently.
   - But is this label pigeonholing me?
   - Seeing friends get promoted, jump ship, or start businesses—what am I doing?

2. **Unclear next steps**
   - Stay in Ops? It feels like there’s nothing new left to explore.
   - Move into Management? I’m not sure if I’m cut out for it, and I have no actual experience.
   - Switch fields? Too vague; I don’t even know where to start.

3. **Mismatch between skills and passion**
   - I’m good at cross-departmental communication, organizing events, and dealing with people.
   - But these skills are becoming increasingly mechanized in my current role.
   - Is it a problem with my abilities, or the position itself?

---

## Potential Directions (To be validated)

**A. Deepen Operations Expertise**
- Shift from executor to strategic thinker.
- Learn hard skills like data analysis and user research.
- But... it feels like staying on the exact same path.

**B. Management Path**
- Lead a team, handle project management.
- Requirements: Understand the day-to-day of management, find opportunities to test the waters, gain relevant knowledge.
- Risk: Not sure if this is what I actually want.

**C. Other Possibilities**
- Product, Ops consulting, creative planning?
- Too vague; need more information.

---

## Immediate Action Items

- [ ] Have a chat with Zhang Ming about career development (not a work report).
- [ ] Talk to friends who have transitioned into management to hear their honest experiences.
- [ ] Look for relevant courses or workshops, specifically regarding management/strategy.
- [ ] Try to proactively take on strategic tasks in my current role to see if I’m genuinely interested.
- [ ] Stop mindlessly scrolling through career advice articles; switch to targeted learning.

---

## Final Thoughts

I’m not dismissing my current job. Stability, recognition, and working with a great team—these are all precious things. But I don’t want to be doing the exact same thing five years from now and asking myself, "Why didn't I try something else back then?"

The question isn't "Should I change?" but rather "What do I want?" I haven't figured that out yet.

But at least today, I’ve admitted it.

Chen Hao, are you still awake?

I just got off the phone with my dad, and I really wanted to share something with you.

Actually, I’ve been reflecting a lot lately, especially about that team communication issue. I kept wondering if I wasn't doing well enough, or if I needed to change who I am. But while chatting with my dad today, he said something that suddenly set me at ease. He said, "I’m so proud to see you growing like this. You aren't running away from difficulties; you are facing them, learning from them, and rising above them."

I told him then that I don't need to change myself; I just need to grow on the foundation of who I already am. My dad laughed and said, "Now that is wisdom."

You know? In that moment, I felt my whole body relax. It’s like I’ve been chasing this image of a "perfect manager," feeling like I had to get everything right. But I realize now that I don’t need to be perfect; I just need to be learning. Making mistakes is normal; what matters is how I learn from them.

The biggest thing I’ve learned this month is to accept my own imperfections. It’s not about giving up; it’s about moving forward in spite of them.

I feel like being with you has helped me learn this, too. You always have this rational way of looking at my anxiety, yet you never make me feel judged. That kind of balance is really rare.

It’s getting late. See you tomorrow? I’d love to tell you more about what’s been on my mind lately.

I still find it hard to believe that yesterday’s speech actually went that smoothly.

I was a nervous wreck from the moment I woke up. I kept obsessively checking my script and even did one last run-through in front of the bathroom mirror. Chen Hao told me I was just wearing myself out, but I couldn't help it. Maybe it’s because this speech meant something different to me—it wasn't just a routine report; it was a chance to showcase my vision as a manager to the entire company.

Ironically, I felt more relaxed once I was actually on stage. Maybe it’s because I truly believed in what I was saying. When I got to the part about "finding the balance between driving business and nurturing team growth," I saw a lot of people nodding, and some were even taking notes. The applause at the end was incredibly warm, and that was the moment I finally let out a sigh of relief.

But the question now is... what comes after the applause?

People have been coming to me all day. The Product Manager asked if I could run a management workshop for their team; the Head of Engineering wants me involved in their organizational development project; even HR sent me an email. Zhang Ming seemed particularly thrilled—he praised me publicly during the meeting, saying I represent the style of the company’s new generation of leaders.

I should be happy about this recognition. And I am. But at the same time... I’m a little panicked.

What do these invitations mean? Does it mean I’m expected to take on more? My own team still has so much that needs my attention. If I spread myself thin across all these projects, will I end up neglecting my core responsibilities? And are people’s expectations of me getting too high? Just because one speech went well, I’m suddenly being treated as a "benchmark." That label feels a bit heavy.

I’m thinking I might need to have a real talk with Zhang Ming. Not to turn down these opportunities, but to get clear on what I actually want. What kind of manager do I want to be? The "all-rounder" who can do everything, or someone who goes deep and stays focused on their own domain?

Yesterday, I said, "driving business while caring for the team." It sounds perfect in theory, but how do you balance that in reality? I can’t be in five places at once and do a great job in all of them.

I talked to Chen Hao about this tonight, and he asked, "Are you anxious about your own success?" I didn't answer right away, but he hit the nail on the head. I always seem to be worrying about the next step instead of just enjoying the current achievement.

Maybe that decision in itself is a sign of progress—I’m no longer the person who says "Sure, I'll give it a try" to everything.

I’ll keep thinking about it tomorrow. My brain is a bit fried right now.

June 23, 2024, Night

I cried while on the phone with my parents.

It wasn't a sudden breakdown; the tears just started falling mid-sentence. Mom asked if I’d been working overtime again. I said yes, and then I couldn't find my voice. Dad took the phone and calmly asked how I’d been lately. So, I told him everything—the pressure from the project, the issues with the team, and how powerless I’ve been feeling.

Dad listened for a long time, then said, "You don't have to carry everything on your own."

My immediate reaction was to argue, to say I wasn't, but deep down I knew that’s exactly what I’ve been doing. I’ve been trying to execute every part of my job perfectly, trying to manage everyone’s feelings at home, and trying to maintain that balance of independence and intimacy in my life with Chen Hao... It feels like I’m doing the work of several people at once.

Then I asked him, "Dad, how are you and Mom doing? I’ve been wanting to ask lately, but I didn't know how to bring it up."

They both said they were fine, but I could tell they were just trying to reassure me. I was direct: I told them I wanted to set up a regular health check-up plan for them, that I could support them financially, and that if they were willing, we could discuss them moving closer to me.

Dad was silent for a moment, then said he didn't want to be a burden.

I told him firmly, "It’s not a burden, Dad. It’s my responsibility as your daughter, and it’s something I *want* to do."

As I said those words, I realized that I wasn't running away from anything, nor was I just checking off a list of obligations. I truly want to take care of them. This feeling is completely different from that old anxiety of "I should be doing better."

After hanging up, I sat there for a long time. I felt exhausted, but also a sense of clarity. Maybe the "perfect balance" I’ve been chasing doesn't exist. Maybe this is just what life is—there are times when you feel powerless, times when you need to ask for help, and times when you take on responsibility for the people who matter. None of that is a failure.

Tomorrow, I need to talk to Zhang Ming about the project and see if we can adjust the timeline. I also need to let Chen Hao know how I’ve been feeling; I need to pull myself together a bit before he gets back from his business trip.

But for tonight, I’ll just let myself be tired for a while.

We talked for over two hours, from his projects to my recent thoughts on my career path, and even why I want to learn dance (he said it’s actually about exploring my own boundaries, and I think he’s right).

Most importantly, he didn’t ask me things like "when are you getting married" 😂 Instead, he asked, "What kind of life do you want most for yourself?"

We’ve made plans to check out an art gallery exhibition this weekend and then just grab a bite somewhere. Nothing forced, just a very natural vibe.

I keep thinking about what you said: "This is so you—rational, yet brave enough to try." That’s exactly how I feel right now. Rationally, I feel this person is worth getting to know, but I’m also brave enough to see where things go. It’s not about meeting expectations anymore; it’s just... something I want.

I feel much better now. Just wanted to share that with you 😊

Wang Fang, are you still awake?

I’ve been lying in bed for almost an hour, and my mind has been racing about my career. I finally just picked up my phone, opened a memo, and started writing a "Career Exploration Journal" (haha, sounds a bit dramatic, but that’s what I’m calling it).

I listed four possible directions: staying in execution, moving into management, starting new projects, or pivoting to consulting. After writing them down, I suddenly realized something strange—I don't need to have the answer right now.

At that exact moment, I felt this incredible sense of peace. It wasn’t because I’d figured out some grand life lesson; it was just that I suddenly accepted the uncertainty. Before this, I’d been so anxious—seeing colleagues get promoted, you doing your rotation, Liu Yang getting married—it felt like everyone was moving forward while I was just stuck in place. That feeling of being left behind is honestly miserable.

But now I realize that maybe this anxiety itself is what’s pushing me to explore, rather than forcing me to make a final decision today. I can keep working while testing out these different paths to see which one actually fits. There’s no rush.

During your rotation, have you ever felt this way? That feeling where things are uncertain, but because you’ve accepted it, you actually feel a bit lighter?

I’m feeling a little wired now, and I’d love to hear your thoughts.

# Diary

**Friday Afternoon, November 3, 2023**

Just got back from the cafe, and my head is still a bit of a blur.

I’m sitting in the office, but I haven't opened my work emails yet. I need to sort through today’s events first, or it feels like they’ll just stay stuck in my chest.

To be honest, I had to force myself to go to that leadership salon this morning. My headspace hasn't been great these past few weeks; the pressure has been so intense I can barely breathe. Regarding that team conflict I’ve been handling—I keep second-guessing myself. Did I do the right thing? Was I too soft? Or was I too harsh? Every decision feels like walking a tightrope.

But... when that senior operations director said, "I've been through the exact same struggle," I actually felt like crying.

It wasn't a cry of self-pity, but rather—the feeling of being seen. Of being understood. She said that when she made her first management decisions, she also struggled with constant self-doubt and felt like she wasn't cut out for it. She said management is essentially something you learn through a continuous cycle of making mistakes, reflecting, and adjusting. No one knows what they’re doing at the start.

I always thought this anxiety was *my* problem. That I wasn't mature enough, decisive enough, or... something enough. But hearing her say that, I suddenly realized—maybe this isn't a flaw; maybe it’s just normal.

We exchanged contact info. She said I could reach out anytime if I had questions. I also added two other managers on WeChat. It feels like I’ve found some "fellow travelers." I don't have to grope around in the dark alone anymore.

When I walked out of the cafe, the sunlight was blinding, but I felt a bit lighter.

Still... I’m a little worried. Is this feeling of being understood just temporary? When I get back to work next week and face new problems, will the anxiety just come rushing back? Am I actually becoming a manager? Or am I just learning how to pretend I’m in control?

Whatever, I’m not going to overthink it right now.

Chen Hao is working late tonight, so I might give Wang Fang a call. She’s been busy with her own stuff these past few weeks, and we haven't had a real talk in a while. I want to tell her about today. And... I want to ask her if she thinks I’ve changed lately. If I’ve become more anxious.

Wait, I also want to video call my parents. It’s been a while since we had a proper chat. Last time we spoke, Mom asked about work, but I just brushed it off with a "it's fine, it's fine" and hung up in a hurry. I regret that a bit. They’ve always been so supportive, yet lately, I’m the one avoiding them.

I’ll deal with it tomorrow. I’m a bit exhausted today.

But at least... at least I’m not on this road alone. That thought makes me feel a lot better.

Fangfang, I have to share something with you haha!

Zhang Ming came to me earlier wanting me to take on a new community operations project. The old me would have definitely just blurted out "Sure, no problem" and agreed right away. But this time, I didn't.

I told him, "I want to check my current workload first before getting back to you." Then this afternoon, I did a careful assessment and realized my current projects have me almost at full capacity. If I added a new one, I’d definitely end up burnt out. So, I sent Zhang Ming an email politely declining, along with my reasons and a few suggestions.

You know what? When I sent that email, I didn't even have that "Am I being uncooperative?" guilt. Instead, I felt... relieved? Like I’ve finally learned how to say the word "no."

I used to automatically agree to everything and then just exhaust myself. Now I realize that saying no is actually a way of being responsible—responsible to myself, and to the quality of my work.

Do you feel like I’ve changed a bit lately? 😄

---

By the way, are you free this weekend? I want to take you to try that new pottery studio. I haven't quite processed everything from my last pottery class yet and want to go play around with it again.

Wang Fang, I need to tell you something. I’m still a bit dazed right now.

I was just on a video call with my dad, and he said something that just... how do I put this? It suddenly made me think of what Zhang Ming told me last time.

My dad said, "Making mistakes is normal; what matters is how you learn from them." You know, that is almost word-for-word what Zhang Ming said to me in the office the other day. When I heard it from Zhang Ming, I was still a bit skeptical—I thought he was probably just trying to comfort me. But hearing my dad say it now, I realized... maybe I’ve had it wrong all along.

I’ve been so focused on being this "perfect manager" who never screws up and keeps everyone happy. But both of them are actually telling me the same thing—being a manager isn't about being perfect; it's about constant learning.

Even my mom chimed in. She said, "We’re proud of you not because you’re perfect, but because you’re always learning and improving." My eyes got a bit misty hearing that, Wang Fang. I was so obsessed with perfection before, and I’ve just been wearing myself out.

I feel like something has been slowly shifting over the last couple of days. It’s not like the pressure has suddenly vanished, but... things feel a bit different?

What do you think? Am I overthinking this?

# Things I Need to Sort Out

**Time:** July 14, 2023, 11:15 PM
**Location:** At home, lying in bed; Chen Hao is already asleep

---

## The Core Issue

What Wang Fang said today keeps looping in my head: "You can't please everyone at the same time. You need to make a choice."

I know she’s right. But the problem is—I really don’t want to let anyone down. It’s not about being dramatic; it’s just... I haven’t figured out how to handle it.

**On Chen Hao’s side:**
- He’s been a bit bothered lately by how I always want to go home on weekends.

- I heard the subtext: he wants more certainty, more "us" time.
- But I... I can’t just abandon my parents either.

**On the work side:**
- Zhang Ming recently asked if I wanted to take on a new project.
- My knee-jerk reaction was, "Let me think about it."
- What I was actually thinking was: Do I really have any energy left?

---

## I Need to Ask Myself Honestly

1. **What do I actually want?**
   - Not "what I should do," but what I truly want.
   - Chen Hao? My parents' peace of mind? Recognition at work?
   - Or... do I just want the feeling that "I’m in control of the situation"?

2. **Can I actually keep the promise of "not letting anyone down"?**
   - Honest answer: No.
   - So why am I still pretending that I can?

3. **What can I actually commit to?**
   - To Chen Hao: I need to tell him what’s on my mind instead of brushing him off with "I’m busy."
   - To my parents: I can offer regular company and a listening ear, but I can’t be their "doctor."
   - To work: I can do my current job well, but I can’t expand indefinitely.

4. **Who do I need to have an honest conversation with?**
   - Chen Hao: Most urgent. He’s waiting for a signal, and I need to give him one.
   - My parents: We need a deep talk about the boundaries of my capabilities.
   - Wang Fang: Thank her for her bluntness; maybe talk one more time.

---

## Next Steps

- [ ] Have a real talk with Chen Hao this weekend—not while watching a show, but sitting down properly.
- [ ] During the next video call with my parents, ask them what they’re actually thinking instead of just asking about their health.
- [ ] Reply to Zhang Ming: Finish current projects first before considering new commitments.
- [ ] Stop acting like I’m "superhuman."

---

Writing this out, I feel a bit tired. But things are also a bit clearer.

It’s not that I’m not good enough, or that I don’t love them enough. It’s that I need to learn to say "no."

Or rather—learn to say, "This is the limit of what I can do."

April 18, 2024, Night

Liu Yang said I’ve changed—that I’ve become warmer.

I froze for a second when she said that to me in the office. Then I realized she wasn’t just paying me a compliment; she was stating a fact she’d observed. It was a strange moment, like suddenly catching a glimpse of myself in a mirror, but seeing the version of me that exists in someone else’s eyes.

When she got married a while back, I took the initiative to help her handle a bunch of things. It wasn’t because I felt like I had to "prove something because I’m still single," but because I genuinely wanted to do something for her. When did this shift happen? I can’t quite remember.

I used to see wedding photos on WeChat Moments and ask myself, "Why am I the only one still single?" I hardly ever ask that anymore. It’s not just because I have Chen Hao now—though having him has certainly changed a lot—it’s more that I seem to have finally understood that the question was wrong to begin with.

Chen Hao and I have talked about the future a few times. Moving in together, getting married, when to have kids... but these conversations feel different now. We’re not racing against time or following some "supposed to" schedule; we’re genuinely asking, "What kind of life do we want?" That question is harder, and much more important.

Work has been going a lot better, too. I no longer feel like I’m just "executing tasks"; I’m actually managing. I’m thinking about how to help my team members grow and how to build an environment of trust. After a report recently, Zhang Ming told me my thinking was very clear. In that moment, I realized I no longer need others to tell me whether I’m good enough.

I’m also making plans with my parents. I might go home this weekend to talk to them about their retirement. I used to think of this as a heavy topic, but now I’m actually looking forward to it. It feels like I can finally face the next stage of life together with them.

When Liu Yang said I’m "warmer," I think what she saw wasn't that I’ve become more outgoing, but that I’ve finally let go of that tension—that constant "need to prove myself." That tension drained me for years.

The "me" right now finally seems to believe that success isn't defined by "whether I'm married," "whether I got promoted," or "what I own." It’s defined by "whether I am living a meaningful life."

This realization came a little late, but it came at just the right time.

# Nov 6, 2022, Evening

Sitting on the sofa, it’s pitch black outside the window. I just finished showering and meant to go to bed early, but my mind is still racing, so I decided to pick up my phone and jot this down.

Lately, things have been really strange. Ever since I attended Mr. Li’s workshop, it’s like I’ve opened a Pandora’s box—I’ve realized just how much I’m lacking. Data analysis, strategic planning, systems thinking... watching others discuss these things makes me start to doubt myself. Am I really cut out for management? Or should I just stick to execution where it feels safer?

My sleep was terrible for a while. I’d often wake up in the middle of the night with thoughts like "I still have so much to learn" or "I might never get this" spinning in my head. I’ve been posting less on WeChat Moments, too; Wang Fang even asked if I was overworked lately. It’s not really exhaustion, though—it’s more like a sense of powerlessness. It’s like being in a dark room and suddenly realizing you can’t see the path beneath your feet.

But something small happened today. Li Xin came to me to discuss a question about user data. She said she noticed I’d been studying data analysis lately and wanted to get my thoughts. My gut reaction was, "I only just started learning this myself; there’s still so much I don’t know." But then we started talking anyway. Some of the ideas I suggested turned out to be helpful to her, and her perspective was eye-opening for me, too.

In that moment, it hit me—I’ve been constantly asking "What am I missing?" but I never stopped to think "What can I share?"

What I can share is the very process of my learning. Not because I’ve mastered anything yet, but because I am genuinely in the middle of it. That seems to have more value than pretending I know everything, doesn't it?

So tonight, I posted that sentence on my Moments: "Professionalism isn't about having all the answers; it's the ability to keep learning and sharing." When I was posting it, I wrote a draft, deleted it, wrote it again... but finally, I decided to put it out there. Because this isn't just some "chicken soup" for the soul; it’s how I truly feel.

I’m not afraid anymore.

It’s not because I’ve suddenly become some expert, but because I finally understand—I don’t need to become someone who knows everything overnight. I just need to stay curious, be willing to learn, and be willing to share my journey. That is enough.

Maybe that’s what "reliability" actually means. It’s not about being perfect; it’s about being honest.

Alright, time for bed. Tomorrow is another day.

# Sunday, September 10, 2023, 10:30 AM

Still can't quite sleep. My mind was racing all night, and it hasn't fully settled down yet.

When Li Xin congratulated me, I caught something in his eyes for a split second... how should I put it? It wasn't that he was unhappy, but something had shifted. Maybe he’s also realizing that from now on, I’m his boss. That feeling of us revising proposals together and complaining about the workflow—it’s probably going to change. When I told him, "I'm a bit nervous," he smiled and said, "You’ve always been the most reliable one."

Reliable. I’ve heard that word so many times. It used to feel comforting; now, it feels... a bit heavy.

Because being a reliable doer and being a reliable manager seem like two different things. For a doer, reliability means getting things done, delivering on time, and avoiding mistakes. But what does reliability mean for a manager? It means making decisions, handling conflicts, and being responsible for the work of five people. I’ve never done that before.

Last night, I pulled up the list of the new team and went through their profiles one by one. As I looked at them, I realized my focus was slowly shifting from "Can I do this?" to "How should I interact with them?" It’s a strange shift, but also a bit... relieving? It’s like I’ve moved from obsessing over my own abilities to thinking about the practical "how-to."

But the problem is, I really don't know how to have authority without losing this... warmth. I’m the kind of person who organizes team outings and listens to colleagues vent. Now, I’m the one assigning tasks and evaluating their performance. How do I balance these two identities?

And what about the critical decisions? What if I make the wrong call? Zhang Ming trusts me, but that’s because I’ve always been consistent. Now that the choices are mine to make, will I suddenly panic?

Chen Hao asked me yesterday, "So, are you happy?" I said yes, but honestly, I feel more... exhausted? No, not exhausted, but pulled in too many directions. Work is going to demand more energy, he’s busy with his own product, and I still need to find time for my parents... Sometimes I wonder, can I really handle all of this at once?

At least I’m not just worrying aimlessly anymore. I’m thinking about specifics—how to handle the first team meeting, how to build trust while staying approachable, and how to be more confident in decision-making. This is slightly better than yesterday, when I was just asking myself, "Can I do this?"

Whatever, coffee first. I need to organize the team’s workflow this afternoon; I need a plan before we meet on Monday.

I can do this. I think.

I was a little nervous in the locker room, worried I wouldn't be able to keep up or that I’d have to explain my month-long disappearance to familiar faces. But actually, no one asked. Everyone just went about the class as usual, and I just danced as usual. It’s strange, but this feeling of being "allowed" to return to my routine was such a relief.

I hopped on a call with Wang Fang after class. She said, "See, you’re doing it right now." I didn't catch her meaning at first, so she added, "You’ve been agonizing over how to choose and how to find a balance. But you don't actually have to choose. You’re just living—doing what you want to do and being with the people you care about."

I felt like crying then, but not the desperate kind. It was more like... a sense of release?

Communication with Chen Hao has been much better lately. We finally laid everything out on the table—he admitted he was actually terrified of losing me, and I told him that what I’m afraid of isn't him, but my own inability to please everyone. He just laughed and said, "You're not Superman, you know." It sounds so simple, but I don't think I had ever truly accepted that.

Then there’s my parents. During our video call last week, I just came out with it—I told them I probably wouldn't be able to stay home for as long as planned in early August. Mom was a bit disappointed, but Dad asked if it was because of work. I told them the truth: I wanted to go on a trip with Chen Hao. To my surprise, they both said, "Well, how about we go too?"

I was totally stunned. I asked if that wouldn't be awkward. Mom said there was nothing to be awkward about; she wants to meet Chen Hao. Dad added, "We’d like to understand more about your life as young people."

So, the plan now is for the four of us to go to the beach together in early August. It sounds a bit crazy, but I realized I’m actually looking forward to it. Not the "I have to make everyone happy" kind of expectation, but a genuine desire to have the people I care about all together.

I’ve been wondering these past two weeks if I’m being too greedy, wanting career fulfillment, romantic companionship, and family closeness all at once. But now I think I understand—these things aren't mutually exclusive. I was just pushing myself too hard, feeling like I had to satisfy everyone perfectly, and as a result, I couldn't do anything well.

Wang Fang said this low point actually gave me a gift: it let me see what is real. I don't have to maintain the facade that "everything is fine." I can say "I'm tired," "I can't do it," or "I need help." Ironically, this has made my relationships with those around me feel much more authentic.

I still have to deal with work stuff tomorrow. I know Zhang Ming has some issues with my recent performance. But at least now, I won't feel completely worthless because of it. I’m just going through a period of adjustment, and that’s perfectly normal.

One step at a time. No need to rush.

I’ve been thinking about something lately: what does true professional maturity look like?

It’s not about being the person who says "yes" the fastest, nor is it about letting work become your entire life. Instead, it’s the ability to know clearly what you want and to express it with honesty.

A while back, an opportunity came my way. My initial reaction was excitement. But once I calmed down, I asked myself a few questions: Would this drag me back into a cycle of endless overtime? Could I still maintain my current pace of life? Is this the kind of growth I actually want?

In the end, I decided that any professional development I pursue must be built on the foundation of "being able to sustain my quality of life." It’s not that I don’t want to progress; it’s that the way I progress must align with how I understand life.

I had an honest conversation with my manager about these thoughts. He listened intently. I was a bit nervous at first, fearing I’d be seen as "lacking ambition." But that’s not it. I just wanted to say: I can do better, but the prerequisite is that I take care of myself.

Also, a big thanks to Chen Hao and Wang Fang for being there lately. It was through our conversations that I truly figured these things out.

# 2023.03.17

I sat in the dark for a long time after getting home tonight. Outside the window is the night view of a tier-one city—it’s a bit noisy, but somehow, I feel a sense of quiet.

What happened today feels like it’s not that simple.

Starting from the drama exhibition, Chen Hao kept asking me questions. Not the "you should do this" kind of advice, but genuine inquiries—"Where do you feel stuck right now?" "What does the difference between operations and management mean to you?" I was a bit defensive at first because I hadn't even figured out the answers myself. But he just listened quietly and kept asking.

I suddenly realized that most people just pity me. When friends hear about my career struggles, they say things like, "But your life is so stable already," or "There’s no need to rush." Chen Hao is different. He actually seems to want to understand what I’m thinking, rather than just comforting me. Being truly "seen" feels completely different.

Later at the cafe, he said, "I want to pursue a serious relationship with you." Just like that—straightforward. I was stunned for a second, and then I told him exactly what I thought. No testing the waters, no ambiguity. It was like two rational people confirming a fact.

I don’t know how to describe the feeling. It wasn't the kind of heart-racing, mind-going-blank sensation. It was more like... relief? Like someone finally saw me—not my social skills or my professional competence, but my thoughts and confusion as a human being.

My friends have been talking about marriage and dating again this month. Before, listening to them felt exhausting, like I was being rushed. But it’s different now. Not because I "finally have a boyfriend," but because I proactively chose this person. I see him for who he is, and he sees me. The feeling is entirely different.

As for my career, I think I’ve cleared some things up too. It’s not that Chen Hao gave me the answers, but through his questions, I mapped it out myself—what I want probably isn't a simple promotion, but more room for reflection in my work. That thought is incredibly clear now.

I haven't told my parents yet. I haven't told Wang Fang either. Right now, this belongs only to me, and I want to sit with this feeling a little longer.

Getting sleepy. More tomorrow.

# Sunday, March 26, 2023, 11:00 AM

What happened last night has been playing on a loop in my head.

Chen Hao said he had to work late and asked if I wanted to change our plans and wait for him. At that moment, I was actually a bit torn. The old me would have immediately said, "No problem, I'll wait for you," and then I would’ve just curled up at home, scrolling through my phone and checking the clock every few minutes. But this time... I didn't.

I told him I was going to check out that new art exhibition and asked if he wanted to join. He said he couldn't make it, so I told him I’d head there first and see him later.

I felt a bit uneasy saying it. Would I come across as inconsiderate? Would he think I didn't care enough about him? But the stronger feeling was—I really wanted to see that show. And I realized that I don't want to give up the things I want to do just to wait for someone.

I felt so good while I was at the exhibition. Walking through the gallery alone, looking at the installation art, it suddenly hit me—this is the lifestyle I truly want. It’s not about changing my rhythm to please someone else; it’s about being able to stick to my own ideas while still being with someone.

Chen Hao ended up coming after work, and we met at the entrance. He said, "I've been wanting to see this for a while," and we walked through it all over again together. He would stop to look closely at certain pieces and occasionally turn to me and ask, "What do you think of this one?" In that moment, I realized this is the kind of relationship I want—two people who have their own thoughts and plans, but who can share and communicate sincerely when they are together.

It’s not about me changing for him, or him changing for me. It’s just... two independent people, choosing to be together.

All that previous anxiety about "still being single at 27" and those questions about "whether I should compromise" seemed to loosen their grip last night. It’s not that I’m finally "complete" because I have a partner; it’s because I found someone who makes me feel more certain about being myself.

It feels really good.

# Project Progress Log - 2022.11.12

**Subject: User Data Visualization Module - Tableau Practical Application**

---

Spent about 3 hours on Tableau tonight and finally managed to get that user retention visualization chart done.

I’d been stuck on the data pivoting part for a while—every time I imported the data, it threw an error, which was pretty frustrating at first. But today was different; whenever a problem popped up, I just took a deep breath and started over instead of giving up. Maybe what I’ve been learning lately is starting to click; I’m not as intimidated by these tools anymore.

The final chart clearly shows the user activity trends over the past three months, categorized by different user segments. March 25, 2024, 8:00 PM

Chen Hao and I just got back from dinner. He’s in the living room watching a game, and I’ve been sitting in the bedroom alone for a while. I want to write down our conversation from tonight before the feeling fades.

We were actually just talking about the logistics of living together. He suggested that we keep our finances separate, and I felt a bit uneasy at first—not that I disagreed, but I felt a sudden pang of apprehension. It felt like something was being defined, or a line was being drawn. We’ve never talked about money so explicitly before.

But I didn’t push back right away. Instead, I asked him why he felt that way. He said it gives us both autonomy so we don't have to report every little thing to each other. As I listened, I realized I was actually drawn to the idea. Not because I want to hide anything from him, but because... I realized I need something that belongs solely to me. Not just money, but the power to decide.

So, I told him that I want at least two nights a week to myself. I could go to a class, meet up with Wang Fang, or do absolutely nothing—just undisturbed time. I was a bit nervous saying it, worried he might feel like I was pushing him away.

He was quiet for a moment, and I thought he was going to be upset. But then he said, "That’s perfectly reasonable." He even started thinking about how to make it work, suggesting we keep a personal calendar so we’re both on the same page.

I was honestly a bit stunned by his understanding. It’s not that I didn’t trust him, it’s just... I’ve been waiting for that moment where I could honestly say "I need space" without it coming from a place of guilt or compromise. He accepted that need and even helped me figure out how to achieve it.

Later, we cuddled on the sofa for a while. He asked me what I was thinking, and I said, "I was thinking that this is what a warm home feels like."

It’s not that there are no conflicts or that everything is perfect. It’s that we are both standing our ground while respecting each other. I don’t have to sacrifice my independence for intimacy, and he doesn’t have to suppress himself for my needs. We are just together, as we are.

It’s a very grounding feeling. It’s not the "I’ve finally found the one" kind of excitement, but rather the certainty of "we are building a way of life that belongs to us."

I’m no longer driven by those "how you should be" expectations. Every choice I’m making is because I truly want to. Being with Chen Hao, asking for personal time, accepting his ideas on finances—it’s all me.

I feel like I’ve finally grown up.

Friday night, December 8, 2023

Dad said I look much more spirited lately.

He just blurted it out while we were on a video call. Mom nodded beside him, adding, "Yeah, you always seemed so rushed before." I was a bit taken aback for a moment, and then I smiled.

I told them I’ve been adjusting my pace lately and found that life has become more meaningful.

As I said those words, I even surprised myself a little—because I truly meant it.

That feeling of being constantly pushed by something seems to have slowly loosened its grip. It’s not that work has become easier or that the pressure has suddenly vanished, but rather... I’ve stopped trying to make everything perfect. I’ve started accepting that sometimes I just get tired, sometimes I make mistakes, and sometimes I just need to stop and rest.

It might not sound like a big deal, but for me, it’s a real transformation.

Deciding to go home at least once a month is part of this process, I guess. It’s not because I "should" spend time with my parents, but because I genuinely want to. I want to hear them talk about the little things, see how the house is doing, and just sit on that familiar sofa doing nothing but chatting with them.

I used to see it as a "responsibility," which came with a side of guilt. It’s different now. It’s become something I proactively want to do.

Chen Hao noticed the change recently, too. The day before yesterday, he said that when I talk to him now, I no longer have that frantic "what should I do" vibe; instead, I’m thinking more about "what I want." He said it makes him feel much more at ease. We even started talking about the future—not questions like "when are we getting married," but "what kind of life do we want." That kind of conversation feels completely different.

I still have a lot of uncertainties. Being a manager is still new to me, and there’s so much to learn. But I’m not afraid of that uncertainty anymore. I’ve started to believe that learning and growing are simply part of the process. I don’t need to become a perfect manager overnight; I just need to take it step by step.

I’ll remember what Dad said for a long time. It confirmed something—that my change is real, and it’s being seen. It’s not self-deception or me romanticizing my own life; someone actually noticed that I’m different.

I think this is where I am right now. Not perfect, but real. Not finished, but on my way.

It feels good.

Wang Fang, I just got off a video call with my parents and finally had a moment to process everything. I really need to talk to you.

I had a proper conversation with them yesterday about their retirement—things like regular checkups, financial planning, and how we’ll be there for them. To be honest, I’d been carrying this constant sense of worry, like a weight in my chest. But this time was different. We actually sat down and worked through things together, turning those vague fears into concrete plans.

The thing that touched me most was something my dad said. He told me, "Don't give up on your own growth for our sake." I was stunned for a second. I always thought being mature meant making sacrifices—that I had to choose between my family and myself. But what he said made me realize that maybe true balance isn't about sacrifice, but... moving forward together?

It’s just like what Chen Hao and I are planning right now. It’s not about me giving something up to accommodate him, or him giving something up for me; it’s about us figuring out what kind of life we want and heading in that direction together.

My mom had a lot of encouraging things to say too, but my dad’s words really hit home. It feels like I’ve finally stopped obsessing over "what I should do" and started actually thinking about "what I want."

You were right—sometimes life’s turning points happen quietly like this. It’s not some sudden, dramatic upheaval, but a moment where things just click.

Are you up for dancing this weekend? I’d love to chat more about this and hear what’s been going on with you lately.

Wang Fang, I have to tell you something—I feel so much lighter after my talk with Zhang Ming.

It’s about that whole work-life balance thing I’ve been struggling with. I’d been debating whether or not to bring it up with him, but this afternoon I just went for it and scheduled a 1-on-1. I told him quite calmly that I want to continue taking on more responsibility, but on the condition that it doesn’t completely squeeze out my personal life. To be honest, I was pretty nervous; I was worried he’d think I wasn’t "committed" enough.

But his reaction totally caught me off guard. He said the best managers aren't the ones working 24 hours a day, but the ones who can actually balance work and life. That one sentence just made all my internal conflict vanish.

I suddenly realized that I’ve been asking "What should I do to be a good manager?" when I should have been asking "What kind of manager do I want to be?" Those are two completely different questions. The first one is passive, but the second one is a choice that’s actually mine.

How about you? Anything been weighing on you at work lately? I get the feeling you’ve been thinking about some career development stuff too. Are you free next weekend? Let’s grab dinner and catch up properly—I’d love to hear your thoughts.

December 5th, Evening

On the subway, it suddenly hit me that I was doing something ordinary yet deeply significant for me—I didn’t open my work group chats.

My phone has been on Do Not Disturb for a week now. No, actually, it’s been two weeks. During the first week, I’d still subconsciously reach for my phone to check for messages; it felt like a smoker catching a whiff of a cigarette while trying to quit. But today, sitting on the train, I opened that book about the meaning of life instead. I actually managed to finish two chapters in one go, without a single shred of guilt.

It sounds like a small thing, but I know it isn’t.

I used to scroll through work chats on the subway, while eating, even while lying in bed. It wasn’t because there was anything truly urgent; it was because I needed to confirm that I was needed. Looking back, it seems almost ridiculous—as if I were using that sense of "being needed" to fill some kind of void.

When I got home, Chen Hao was already cooking. I put down my bag and walked into the kitchen without opening my laptop first or checking for missed emails. I just... stood there, talking to him. We talked about completely pointless things—a ridiculous meeting his product team had today, a funny ad I saw on the subway, what to have for dinner.

When he asked how my day was, I actually answered with a light tone, instead of habitually launching into a vent about work.

I think I’ve finally realized something. It’s not that work isn't important, it’s just that... I don’t need work to prove my worth. I can be a good manager and someone who knows how to relax at the same time. Those two things aren't mutually exclusive.

Turning off the computer and setting my phone to Do Not Disturb—these small actions are actually a way of telling myself: you deserve a life of your own. Not as a reward, not after every single task is finished, but right now. Just as you are.

There are meetings tomorrow and a mountain of things waiting for me. But tonight, I’m just Xiaoyu. Not "Reliable Xiaoyu" or "Manager Xiaoyu." Just Xiaoyu.

It feels really good.

Woke up from a nap on the high-speed train to a blur of green outside the window.

Mom just texted saying Dad’s check-up results are out. Nothing serious, he just needs to watch his diet. The moment I saw that message, I felt a sudden wave of relief. I’ve spent the past week digging through so much information and listing out a mountain of advice for them—I guess I went a bit overboard. Mom said I’m acting like a doctor 😅

Chen Hao asked me why I’ve been heading home every single weekend lately. I couldn't really explain it well, it’s just… some things can’t wait. It’s not that work isn’t important, it’s just that sometimes you have to be clear about what truly matters to you.

A friend mentioned I’ve become quieter lately and asked if work is burning me out. Maybe. Or maybe I’m just learning to let go of certain things to hold onto others.

There’s no perfect way to "be there" for someone. But doing something is always better than doing nothing at all.

# November 20, 2022, 7:00 PM

And now? When I talk about learning data analysis, my tone says "I’m progressing," not "I’m making up for a deficiency." When did that shift happen?

But now I can see clearly that the anxiety isn't telling me "you can't do this"; it's pushing me to "think more carefully." That really changes everything. I used to treat anxiety as evidence of self-doubt; now I treat it as a source of focus. It sounds a bit crazy, but it works.

I’m thinking that maybe I really can pursue a management path. Not because I’ve suddenly become a genius, but because I’ve discovered a pattern: my "reliability" isn't my ceiling—it's my foundation. On top of that foundation, if I add strategic thinking, data literacy, and everything I’ve learned over these eight weeks, I might actually become a different kind of manager. Not the "ivory tower" type, but the kind who can execute, think, and lead people all at once.

I’m going to talk to Zhang Ming about my career planning tomorrow. I used to wait for him to bring it up, but now I want to take the initiative. Not because I’m suddenly overflowing with confidence, but because I have concrete ideas now, rather than just vague anxiety.

Cheers to the challenges of the next stage. I said it tonight, and writing it down now, I want to confirm it again—I truly believe I can keep going.

This feeling—I want to remember it.

Later, Wang Fang asked me privately, "You guys really click, don't you?" and I said, "Yeah, we really do." I was a bit surprised by my own words because they sounded so... certain. It wasn't tentative, nor was it said to meet someone else's expectations. It was just a fact.

For a long time, it felt like I was waiting for something—waiting for the perfect moment, waiting for all the conditions to align, waiting for myself to become a certain version of me. What my mom said hit even harder: "We’re proud of you not because you’re perfect, but because you’re constantly learning and improving." I sat there feeling like I might cry, but not out of sadness. It was more like... the feeling of being seen? Of being understood?

I realized I’ve been obsessed with "getting it right," as if only perfect execution deserves recognition. But they’re operating on a different logic—that growth itself is what’s worthwhile. The difference between those two mindsets is massive.

Sitting there listening, it suddenly hit me—this might be the most rewarding thing I could hear as a manager. It wasn’t about "hitting targets," but rather, "I was seen, and I was trusted."

Over the past two years, I’ve been reflecting on what good management actually looks like. It’s not about using power to suppress others, nor is it about using likability to please them. It’s about truly seeing everyone’s potential and giving them the space to make mistakes and grow. Hao’s promotion feels like a validation of my approach, more so than any performance metric ever could.

Watching more and more people in the team find their own rhythm—not just ticking off tasks, but truly growing—is a feeling that’s hard to describe. It’s like watching something you planted finally start to bloom.

When I mentioned this to Chen Hao tonight, he smiled and said, "You’ve finally stopped working just to prove yourself." He’s right. I care much more now about what we are building together, rather than what I can achieve alone.

So happy for Hao! 🎉 And also relieved to have found a clearer sense of direction for myself.

I’ve been lying in bed for nearly two hours now, and my head is still a mess.

The complex interface, the dense rows of function buttons, all those terms I’d never seen before… I stared at the screen for five minutes and then just shut it down. Sitting there in my chair, I told myself, "Maybe this really isn't for me."

The moment those words came out, I felt a pang of regret. But that’s exactly how I felt at the time—a very real, undeniable sensation.

After dinner, I lay in bed thinking I’d just let it go. After all, the workshop has plenty of other sessions; maybe something later on will be a better fit. But… I kept tossing and turning, unable to sleep. That Tableau interface kept flashing in my mind, and I started asking myself: Why give up? Giving up just because I don't understand it at first—isn't that what I’ve always done?

I dragged myself out of bed and opened the software again.

 the next two hours were… rough. I found a tutorial video, followed along, and then tried to do it on my own. At least three or four times, I wanted to just quit. I kept repeating "I have to learn this" in my head, but honestly, it sounded more like I was forcing myself to stay motivated rather than actually feeling confident.

For a moment, I really wanted to call Wang Fang and ask her if I was overthinking things, or if I had simply overestimated myself. But I didn't. I just sat there, clicking buttons over and over, trying to make sense of those data tables.

It’s 1:30 AM now. I’m exhausted, my eyes are burning, but my brain is still spinning with data. When I close my eyes, I can see those tables floating in front of me.

I learned some of the basics—not much, but something. But I didn't give up.

What does that prove? I don’t know. Does it mean I have grit? Or does it mean I’m just deluding myself, forcing myself to do something I’m not cut out for?

I’m really tired. I have work tomorrow.

But I remember Mr. Li saying in class that what a manager needs isn't talent, but the willingness to keep learning. At the very least, tonight I proved… I have that willingness?

I’m not sure. But I didn't give up. For now, that’s enough.
