# Saturday, March 18, 2023

Sitting in a cafe with the book Chen Hao recommended, but I keep spacing out.

Yesterday’s drama exhibition is still looping in my head. It’s not that "butterflies in my stomach" feeling; it’s more like... a sigh of relief? Like a string that’s been pulled taut for a long time has finally slackened.

He said the stage design was interesting—not just flashy for the sake of it, but because every detail was telling a story. We just stood there in the gallery and talked for half an hour, moving from stage design to urban planning, and then to certain moments in life. He doesn’t interrupt or rush to voice his own opinions; he just naturally catches my thoughts and then says something I hadn't considered, but that sounds exactly right.

This is completely different from the people I’ve dated before. It’s not that "he’s great, so I should like him" feeling. It’s more like—he just *gets* it. He understands what I’m saying, and he understands why I’m saying it.

Li Xin asked me at the office today if I’ve started dating someone. I didn’t deny it. I just told her quite frankly that there’s been "a little pleasant surprise." She gave me a huge smile, and I didn't feel embarrassed at all. Instead, I felt like this isn't something that needs to be hidden, but something natural and worth sharing.

After I posted that photo on WeChat Moments, some friends asked "Who is it?" in the comments, but I didn't reply right away. It’s not about being intentionally mysterious; it’s just... I want to observe a bit longer. I want to make sure this isn't just wishful thinking on my part.

Actually, I’ve been thinking lately about why being single at 27 is treated like such a problem. It’s not that I don’t care at all, but that previous pressure—from friends, from home, from all those "time to get married" voices on social media—doesn't feel as heavy now. It’s not because I’ve "solved" the problem by finding someone, but because... I’ve realized I’m making an active choice rather than just reacting passively.

If I hadn't met Chen Hao, I might still be asking myself "Why am I still alone?" But now, the questions have become "Is this person really that interesting?" and "Can we go further together?" These two sets of questions feel completely different.

That Management Workshop gave me a lot to think about and made me feel like I was growing. Now, Chen Hao’s appearance feels like a further confirmation that this growth is real. I’m no longer just someone who executes tasks and maintains the status quo. I can feel, I can choose, and I can have a deep conversation with someone who truly understands me.

But I also know I’m still observing. It’s not a lack of trust; it’s just my rational side still at work. I want to see what he’s like under pressure, hear his thoughts on deeper issues, and confirm that this comfort is built on something real.

Mom asked me this week if there was any news. I just smiled and said, "I'll tell you once I've figured it out." She didn't push, which is what I love most about her.

For now, I just want to experience this process. No rush to define it, no rush to tell the world. Just let it happen naturally and see where it goes.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s the kind of tired where you feel full, not the kind where you feel drained.

Another ordinary week.

Sitting in the office on a Friday afternoon, looking at a screen full of emails and to-dos, I suddenly felt a wave of exhaustion. It’s not physical fatigue, but that kind of "I feel like I’m just repeating yesterday" kind of tired.

I’ve been reading some articles about career transitions lately, and one point really hit home: **"The comfort zone is like the frog in boiling water; you think you’re swimming, but you’re actually being slowly cooked."** I always thought I was doing pretty well—colleagues say I’m reliable, my boss recognizes my work, and projects are always finished on time... but that seems to be it. Just "finished," nothing more.

I don’t know when it started, but I’ve realized I’m just doing the same things over and over. Planning events, cross-departmental communication, collecting feedback... a never-ending loop. Sometimes I find myself spacing out in meetings, thinking, "And then what? What comes next?"

Still going to that dance class with Wang Fang this weekend. I’ll probably vent about all these messy thoughts then. Maybe I just need to talk to someone and untangle my mind.

I haven't shared much in the group chats lately. It’s not that there’s nothing interesting to share, it’s just... I don’t really feel like saying anything. It’s a strange feeling.

Anyway, let’s just get through this week first. Some questions might just need time to answer themselves.

# Phone Memo

**Reflections on Today’s Class**

---

**Q1: Why did I get stuck on the question "How to use data to validate decisions"?**

A: Because I’ve never done it systematically. For the past three years, my decision-making logic has basically been: check user feedback → rely on gut feeling/experience → execute. The upside is quick reaction time, but the downside is... I have no way to explain to my superiors *why* a decision is right, other than saying "I feel like it is."

I was genuinely stunned when the instructor asked. In that moment, I realized I might have been using "strong execution" to mask the fact that my "strategic thinking" is weak.

---

**Q2: Do I actually lack data analysis skills? Or have I just never been asked to use them?**

A: Probably both. In my current role, no one really pushes me to do deep data analysis. As long as the events are executed well, user feedback is positive, and KPIs are met, that’s enough. But if I want to move into management, this blind spot will become a real problem.

Zhang Ming once called me "stable and reliable." It sounded like a compliment at the time, but today it hit me—it might also mean I haven't demonstrated a higher level of thinking.

---

**Q3: What should I learn? Where do I start?**

A: 
- Get the basic concepts of data analysis straight first (don't need to be an expert, but need to understand the logic).
- Review a few key projects from the past three years and try to re-analyze them from a "data validation" perspective.
- See if I can find the underlying data-driven logic behind the decisions I made "by experience" back then.
- Chat with Li Xin to see how the Product team justifies their decision-making.

---

**Q4: Does this mean I’m not cut out for management?**

A: I don’t know. But at least now I know what I’m missing, which is better than just moping around feeling like "maybe I’m just not good enough."

After hearing me out today, Wang Fang asked, "So, are you giving up on the idea of management?" I said, "No, I just found a hole I need to patch." She said, "Then go patch it. Stop overthinking."

She’s right. It’s not that I can’t do it; I just need to learn.

---

**To-Learn List:**
- [ ] Find one or two books on data-driven decision-making.
- [ ] Grab coffee with Li Xin next week to ask how Product prioritizes requirements.
- [ ] Put together a "Decision Review" of past projects to look for data-backed clues.
- [ ] Ask the instructor for recommended online courses or resources.

---

9:30 PM. Still a bit anxious, but also... excited?

It feels less like "I’m not good enough" and more like "I’ve found the direction for my next step."

June 19, 2023, 11:00 PM

Insomnia again. I’ve been lying here for half an hour, but my mind is still racing.

When I video-called Mom today, she wanted to tell me about her "work"—it sounded like she and Dad are thinking about taking some classes at a university for seniors. But as soon as I heard that hint of fatigue in her voice, I cut her off to ask how she’s been sleeping and if those hot flashes have come back. Mom gave a helpless little laugh and said, "Oh, you... I wanted to hear how *you* were doing."

I just froze. I knew she was trying to change the subject, that she wanted to hear about my job and the fun things in my life like she used to. But I couldn't stop myself. I fired off a barrage of questions—should she go to the hospital for a checkup? Was she taking the medicine the doctor prescribed? Was the poor sleep a menopause thing? Mom eventually said, very gently, "Alright, alright, I’ll be careful. Don’t worry so much," and then we ended the call.

It wasn't until I put my phone down that I realized how strange I was being.

For the past few days, my brain has been on a loop, replaying the facts that Dad’s blood pressure is high and Mom isn’t sleeping well. It’s like I’ve suddenly been jolted awake—they really are getting old. How did I not realize this so clearly before? We video-call every week, yet it feels like I’ve been subconsciously ignoring something. They are aging, bit by bit, while I’ve been busy chasing a promotion, busy with my new relationship with Chen Hao, busy with all these events...

Looking back now, it feels like I’ve turned my concern for them into an obsession. It’s not a gentle kind of companionship; it’s more like... anxiety? I’m trying to prove I still care by asking a million questions and gathering information. But in reality, I’m not giving them any actual help; I’m just sitting here drowning in guilt and worry.

The pressure from the new project at work is still there, and Chen Hao is busy this week too, so we’ve barely seen each other. Now, add to that the guilt I feel toward my parents. I feel like I’m being pulled in several different directions, with every part of me screaming, "I need time."

What should I do? Keep giving my all at work to prove I deserve that promotion? Or slow down to spend more time with my parents? Or... actually, there is no "or," because I don’t want to give up any of it.

But if that’s the case, I’m just going to keep being this exhausted.

I’ll think about it tomorrow. Right now, I just want to sleep.

Maybe for the next stage of my career, I really am ready to give management a shot.

Friday, September 22, 2023 | Past 11:00 PM

I’m sitting on the bed; Chen Hao is already asleep. The room is quiet, save for the occasional sound of cars passing by downstairs.

At work today, I spent the entire afternoon staring blankly at my computer screen. Emails were piling up. Li Xin asked me about a few details for next week’s event, and I was slow to reply—even a bit impatient. That’s not like me. I know that’s not like me.

But I’m just... stuck.

During the morning meeting, I had to make a decision regarding our user retention strategy. It wasn’t even a major decision—just whether or not to adjust the participation threshold for an activity. Usually, I can make a call like that instantly, but today I just sat there, my mind looping the same questions: Is this the right move? Will the team think this is a stupid choice? Am I about to screw something up again?

In the end, I had to ask Zhang Ming. He looked a bit surprised, since this was something I should have been able to decide on my own. Seeing his expression only made me feel worse.

Wang Fang asked me out for afternoon tea this weekend, but I turned her down. I told her I wasn’t feeling well. It’s not that I’m sick, exactly; it’s just... I don’t want to see anyone. I don’t want to see my friends, and I don’t want to hear about how their jobs or relationships are going. I feel like a loser, like I’d just drag down the whole mood of the gathering.

I know that’s a ridiculous thought. I know Wang Fang doesn’t see me that way. But I just can’t help it.

Chen Hao asked me last night what’s been going on lately, saying I look exhausted. I didn’t give him a real answer; I just said work’s been busy. He didn’t push it, but I can feel his worry. We’re both pretending everything is fine, but we both know it’s not.

I don’t know if I’m really cut out for management. I used to think I was pretty good at what I did—I could organize events, communicate well, and I was reliable. But now, it feels like none of that is enough. What does management require? Decisiveness? Authority? I don’t even trust my own decisions right now; how am I supposed to lead others?

I wonder what would happen if I went back to my old position. At least back then, I knew what I was doing. I knew how to execute a plan and how to collaborate with colleagues. I didn’t have to make the kinds of decisions that leave me doubting myself every single day.

But I also know I shouldn’t think like that. It feels too much like running away.

It’s past 11:00 PM now, and I have to go into the office tomorrow. I should sleep, but I can’t. My mind is still racing, still circling back to those same questions.

I’m tired. Not just physically tired—I’m truly exhausted. It’s the kind of exhaustion that comes from being pulled in every direction and feeling like you’re failing at all of them.

Whatever. I should try to sleep. I hope tomorrow is a little better.

Attended a management training session at work today, and my brain feels completely fried. 😅 The transition from an individual contributor to a manager is much more complex than I imagined. But I guess it’s this "uncomfortable" feeling that lets me know I’m actually growing.

One quote from Mr. Li really stuck with me: "Management isn't about power; it's about responsibility." I was scribbling notes like crazy, terrified of missing anything important. I even went out and bought a few management books this afternoon, planning to dive into them this weekend. It feels a bit like being back in college—that curiosity for a brand-new field is finally back.

New challenges, new beginnings. I don’t know if I’m making all the right moves, but at least I’m putting in the work. Huge thanks to Zhang Ming for the trust, and to my friends for always having my back. 💪

A bit exhausted, but also pretty excited. It’s a complicated feeling.

# Private Chat with Wang Fang

Wang Fang, I need to talk to you about something.

I received a thank-you email from Wang Hao today. He said the feedback I gave him earlier was a huge help in moving his project forward. Then Zhang Ming also had a chat with me—he mentioned he wants to nominate me for the company’s management development program, which is basically a track to prepare for higher-level roles.

Normally, I should be thrilled, right? But when I got that email, I actually paused. I started thinking: these are all great opportunities, but what is it that I actually want?

You know, I’ve realized I’m not really driven by the idea of "how high I can climb" anymore. Instead, I care more about what kind of manager I want to become. Wang Hao’s email didn't make me feel like "oh, my promotion is looking good"; it made me feel like "my management philosophy is having a real impact on someone." That’s a completely different feeling.

I’m thinking that instead of rushing to grab every promotion that comes my way, I should first figure out what kind of professional rhythm I want. I want to help the team grow, but I also want a life. I want to drive the business, but I don’t want to be the kind of manager whose work consumes their entire existence. I think the best managers are the ones who find a "sustainable rhythm," not necessarily the ones who grind the hardest.

It’s a bit like what you told me recently about that workshop instructor—sometimes we think an opportunity is the answer, but we actually need to ask ourselves first, "Is this what I really want?"

I haven’t replied to Zhang Ming yet. It’s not that I’m hesitating about whether to join, but I want to get clear on my true purpose for participating. If it’s just for the sake of a promotion, then it’s not that meaningful. But if it’s about learning how to be a better, more conscious manager, then it’s worth it.

What do you think?

Chen Hao, I’m feeling a bit overwhelmed right now and really need to talk to you.

I was just on a video call with my dad, and he asked me, "Now that you’re managing people, isn't it exhausting?"

It made me realize that I haven't actually stopped to think about that. When I got the promotion, all I could think about was how to hit the ground running and how to earn the team's trust. I never really considered... what all of this actually means.

You know, I’ve been asking myself a really silly question lately: Can I actually be a good manager, a good girlfriend, and a good daughter all at the same time?

Back when I was just an individual contributor, work was work—I could leave it behind the moment I got home. But it’s different now. My head is constantly filled with team issues, decisions, and how to navigate relationships with five different people...

And I’ve noticed that I haven't been there for you as much. I’ve been working late so often, and even on weekends, my mind is still on work. Yesterday, when you mentioned wanting to see that exhibition, I was literally sitting there thinking about the team’s onboarding plan.

I’m not trying to make excuses; I’m just genuinely feeling the pressure. It’s not the work itself, it’s more like... I feel like I’m being pulled in too many directions at once.

What’s your take on this? Am I overthinking things?

I really need to hear your thoughts. You’ve gone through this transition yourself as a PM—how did you handle it?

I also want to know if you think this will affect us. I don’t want to become distant because of work, but right now, I truly feel like I’m running out of bandwidth.

# Discussion Checklist Regarding Moving In Together

**Date**: January 15, 2024 (Evening)  
**Context**: Chen Hao brought up the topic of moving in together tonight. I didn't give an immediate answer, but I’m already giving it serious thought. I’ve decided to jot down the points we need to discuss so that our next conversation can be more organized.

---

## Topics to Discuss with Chen Hao

### 1. Financial Split
- How will we split the rent? (50/50, proportional to income, or one person covering the majority?)
- How will we handle groceries, utilities, and internet bills?
- Should we keep separate accounts plus a joint account?
- How will we handle unexpected expenses?

### 2. Lifestyle & Space
- His routine (I know he sometimes works late) vs. mine.
- How will we divide household chores? (This must be clarified upfront to avoid "hidden expectations.")
- What are our respective needs for personal space?
- Frequency and protocol for friends visiting.

### 3. Timeline & Logistics
- When do we start house hunting? (Before or after Lunar New Year?)
- Target areas? (Considering both our commutes.)
- What lease term is appropriate? (1 year or 2 years?)
- Moving schedule and division of labor.

### 4. Expectations for the Future
- Is moving in together a step toward marriage, or just a desire to live together?
- If it’s toward marriage, what is the rough timeframe?
- Shared lifestyle expectations (e.g., frequency of travel, attending events together, etc.).
- Have we thought about kids? If so, when?

---

## Practical Factors for Me to Consider Individually

### Financial Aspect
- Can my current savings cover the move + security deposit + initial setup costs?
- How much are my monthly expenses expected to increase after moving in?
- Is it necessary to adjust my savings plan?

### Balance Between Work and Family
- Will moving in affect how often I go home to visit my parents?
- Do I need to communicate this to my parents in advance? When is the right time?
- Moving in doesn't mean giving up time with my parents—I need to be clear on this.

### Family Expectations
- How will my parents view this? (They’ll likely be supportive, but I should be prepared.)
- Should they meet Chen Hao’s family first, or wait until after we move in?
- Is it possible they will automatically equate moving in with marriage?

---

## My Thoughts (Current State)

Actually, I don’t feel particularly anxious about this; it feels very natural. It’s not because I feel "it’s time to get married" or because of social pressure, but because I truly want to share a life with Chen Hao. He is a reliable person, and our rhythms match well.

However, I’m well aware that living together isn't just about romance—it’s about reality. That’s why I need to think through and discuss all the practicalities clearly. Doing so will actually make our relationship more stable.

---

## Next Steps

- [ ] Find a good time this week to discuss these topics in detail with Chen Hao (not tonight; need to allow time for reflection).
- [ ] Organize my finances and look at a post-move budget.
- [ ] Think about when and how to bring this up with my parents naturally.
- [ ] If we decide to move forward, start browsing rental listings in target areas on Xiaohongshu or Douban.

---

**Final Thoughts**: Writing this down, I realize how calm I actually feel. There’s no "I need to hurry up and get married" anxiety, nor any "is this too fast" hesitation. I just know exactly what I want and am planning for it methodically. It’s a good feeling.

Some unexpected things happened within the team lately that caught me a bit by surprise.

Li Xin came up to me and asked if I had attended that management training, saying, "I feel like your thinking is much clearer now." To be honest, I was a bit stunned when I heard that—mostly because I feel like I'm still just fumbling my way through, haha.

But thinking about it, it seems like some things really are slowly changing. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become some expert; it’s more that I’ve started to accept the fact that I’m still learning. I used to feel like I had to get everything right, but now I’m more willing to ask, "How can I do better this time?" It actually feels a bit more liberating.

I’m most grateful for the team’s trust and feedback. Without their patience, I wouldn’t have made it this far. Management, it seems, is something you slowly figure out through these kinds of back-and-forth interactions.

Let’s keep at it. 💪

# March 14, 2023, 7:00 PM

Sitting on the sofa, watching the sky darken outside the window. I just finished my shower, and my phone is still sitting there with that message from Chen Hao: "I had a great time today. Same time next week?"

I wonder what’s going on in my head.

When I went to that dinner with Wang Fang and the others, I didn't expect anything special to happen. It was just a normal get-together; I did what I always do—organized things and kept the atmosphere lively. But that one thing Chen Hao said has been looping in my mind: "I think you’re already doing something meaningful. Your understanding of users and the heart you put into your work—that has value in itself."

I didn't expect to be *seen* like that.

Lately, I’ve been overthinking my career—whether to move into management or change directions entirely. The deepest source of my anxiety is always: Is what I’m doing actually meaningful? Am I just repeating tasks? But when he said that, he wasn't praising my achievements or my job title; he was saying... he saw the intrinsic value in the work itself. That feeling of being understood—it’s truly different.

The strangest part is how it has relieved this invisible pressure I’ve been carrying. Before, I’d worry about my friends getting married, or my parents' expectations; I felt like I was being pushed forward by some external force. But this time is different. With Chen Hao, I don't feel that desperate sense of "finally, someone wants me." Instead, it’s a sense of relief: "Oh, so people with similar interests and values actually exist."

This feels like a choice I am making for myself.

We talked for a long time—about everything from product design to art, from work stress to life goals. He asked why I liked learning pottery, and I actually went on and on about it. He wasn't just politely listening, either; he was genuinely reflecting and responding. It reminded me that this is exactly what I’ve been looking for: someone who can discuss interesting things with me, who understands why I make certain choices, and who finds that balance between logic and emotion.

I haven't told Wang Fang or the others about what’s happened since. I haven't decided when to tell my parents, either. But I’m not in a rush. Unlike before, I don't feel this urge to lock something down or get an immediate result. Right now... I just want to keep talking to him and see where this goes.

It’s a bit strange, but I feel incredibly grounded. At work, I know I’m doing something meaningful, and in my personal life, I’ve found someone who understands me. These two things combined make me feel like I’m finally not being pushed along by life, but that I’m actually in control of it.

Back to work tomorrow. But the "me" right now feels truly different from the "me" of two months ago.

# Sept 12, 2022, 10:15 PM

The lights are off. I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a while now.

Today really took a toll on me. This afternoon, I was working on the post-workshop exercises—specifically the Pivot Table operations—and I was stuck for a solid 30 minutes. Thirty minutes. I kept repeating the same steps over and over, staring at the error messages on the screen, my fingers practically pounding the keys. In the end, I still couldn't fix it. I had to give up and ask for help in the group chat.

In that moment, I really wanted to cry.

It wasn't because I couldn't master an Excel function—I know how trivial that sounds—it was the sudden, crushing weight of powerlessness. I’ve always thought I was doing okay; I’ve worked for so many years and usually pick things up quickly. But today felt like someone suddenly dragged me out of my comfort zone and threw me into a place where I just don't belong.

Then, I did something really stupid. I started searching. "Career change for women at 35," "How hard is it to move from operations to management," "Mid-life career crisis"... The more I read, the more doomed I felt. The posts were all about how hard it is to switch fields, how it gets harder the older you get, how all the opportunities are being snatched up by younger people, the "35-year-old glass ceiling"... I know these things are meant to be alarmist, but I still clicked on them, one by one.

What am I even doing?

I was so excited when the workshop started. I told Wang Fang this was my breakthrough; I discussed management strategies with Chen Hao; I was even eager to try out the new things I’d learned at work. And now? Now I’m sitting here in the dark asking myself: Am I overestimating myself? Can I actually learn this stuff?

My body is tired, and my heart is tired too. It’s not the kind of tired you feel after a long day at work. It’s... I don't know how to describe it. It’s the exhaustion of suddenly realizing you might not be as capable as you thought, that you might be heading in the wrong direction, and only realizing it when it feels too late.

I have work tomorrow. I have to pretend like everything is fine.

I don't want to talk to anyone about this.

## 📋 Key Points to Observe This Week

- [ ] Is there still visible resentment or coldness after the meeting?

### 3. Communication Efficiency

- [ ] Does anyone feel like I’m playing favorites?
- [ ] Does the new division of labor solve the root cause, or is it just a temporary "truce"?
- [ ] Are there other hidden team tensions that I haven't noticed yet?

## 💭 Honest Thoughts

Honestly, I just want to know one thing—did this decision actually work? It felt okay during the meeting, but the second I turned around, I started second-guessing myself. Am I overthinking this?

My biggest fear is that everything looks fine on the surface, but they’re still simmering with resentment and just putting on a front for me. If that’s the case, I’ve really failed.

Or, on the flip side, maybe I shouldn't have made a decision so quickly. Maybe I should have observed for a few more days and listened more to their individual perspectives... but then would I have looked too indecisive?

Whatever. Let’s just see how this week goes. If the team atmosphere feels even slightly better by Friday, at least it means we’re heading in the right direction.

July 24, 2023, Night

I’m sitting on the sofa with my phone on my lap, my mind still a bit of a blank.

When I called my parents just now, I was braced for a lecture. I had rehearsed how I’d explain why I couldn't make it home as planned, and how I’d admit to "letting them down." But their reaction completely caught me off guard.

"Xiaoyu, we can tell you’ve been under so much pressure lately," Mom said.

Then Dad chimed in: "Your mom and I talked it over. We’re both in good health right now, and we really don’t need you to come back so often. You should focus on taking care of yourself."

My eyes welled up right then and there. It wasn't because they agreed, but because... I suddenly realized that the "guilt of not being there" might have been entirely my own creation. They never once said they needed me around that frequently. It was me—I was the one constantly demanding it of myself, constantly feeling like I should be doing more.

Mom also said that instead of me wearing myself out traveling back and forth every month, we should find a more sustainable way. We decided to switch to a video call every two weeks and a visit home once a quarter. She said that would actually be more meaningful.

I’m... I’m honestly stunned by this shift.

These past few days, I’ve been thinking that because I can’t meet everyone’s needs at once, I’m a failure. Chen Hao, work, my parents... I felt like I was letting everyone down. But now I realize that maybe it’s not that I’m incompetent; it’s that my definition of "being there" was flawed. I thought it meant being physically present as much as possible, but my parents told me that what they actually want is for me to be okay.

I’m still processing this. It feels like that trapped, powerless sensation finally has a small crack in it, letting a little light through.

To be honest, I’m still a bit scared. Scared that this sense of relief is only temporary, and scared that once I’m back at work, I’ll be suffocated by the pressure again. But at least for today, I don’t feel like a total failure anymore.

Maybe I need to have a real talk with Chen Hao. I’ve been so cold to him lately, and it’s not his fault.

# Dec 22, 2023, 7:00 PM

Sitting on the sofa, listening to Chen Hao simmering soup in the kitchen, I’m holding my notebook and looking at what I just wrote. I can hardly believe these words came from me.

This afternoon, Chen Hao and I sat down to plan out 2024. It was supposed to be a casual chat, but the deeper we talked, the more intense it got. He asked me, "Do you actually want that promotion?" I froze. I realized my answer wasn't "yes"—it was "I don't know." So, we started breaking it down: what do I actually want?

It wasn't until I put pen to paper that I understood. It’s not that I don’t want to move up; it’s that what I’m looking for has changed. It’s no longer that desperate hunger to "reach a certain level." Instead, I want to deepen my professional expertise, see the people I mentor grow, and drive the business forward while maintaining a healthy team rhythm. It might not sound "ambitious" in the traditional sense, but as I wrote it down, I felt a profound sense of fulfillment. This is what I truly want.

Then, I did something a bit crazy—I divided my notebook into four columns: Career, Personal Growth, Family, and Relationship. Under each, I scribbled down specific thoughts. I used to constantly oscillate between these areas, feeling like focusing on one meant failing the others. But today, as I wrote, I suddenly embraced a new perspective: they aren't in competition; they are different dimensions of my life. Some months, career will take up more space; some months, I’ll want to spend more time with my parents; other times, Chen Hao and I will need more quality time... That’s not failure. That’s just life.

In the family column, I wrote down some specific visions—like taking my parents somewhere they’ve always wanted to go next summer. Not just hitting tourist spots, but truly being present with them. Regular health check-ups, learning something new together. Looking at these ideas, I felt a sense of responsibility—not the heavy kind, but the warm kind.

When Chen Hao brought the soup out and saw me smiling, he asked what I was thinking. I told him, "I think I’ve finally stopped feeling so anxious." He sat down, looked over my notes, and said, "This doesn't look like a plan; it looks like a manifesto." I like that.

And it’s true. This isn't one of those "how to schedule Monday through Friday" plans. This is me saying: I know what kind of life I want, I know who I want to be, and I don't need to be perfect—I just need to be clear.

Sometimes work takes up more time, sometimes being with family is the priority, and sometimes I just want to curl up on the sofa with you and watch a bad movie. All of those are okay, and none of them should come with guilt.

I feel like this shift has also changed how I look at "our future." Instead, I asked her, "Regarding that idea you mentioned last time, how exactly would we optimize it?" Then, I just listened. I truly just listened, only pausing to ask two follow-up questions.

After the meeting, a new team member suggested something that slightly conflicted with our current plan. The old me probably would have said directly, "We’ve already decided on this." But today, I said, "Let’s look at the data together and analyze the differences between the two options." In the end, we merged the two ideas and made a much better decision.

The strangest part was that while doing all this, I didn't feel that familiar tension—that pressure of "I have to prove I'm a competent manager." Instead, I felt... relaxed?

Before heading home, Li Xin walked over and said, "I feel like your thinking has been so much clearer lately. Have you been doing some kind of training?" I smiled and said no. But inside, I was thinking—I didn't go to any training; I just... accepted that I am still learning.

Since I started this management role a month ago, I’ve been constantly at odds with myself. I felt like I should know everything, be able to decide everything, and never make a mistake. But after a few talks with Chen Hao and my dad, and that honest conversation with Zhang Ming, I’m slowly starting to understand—no one is born a perfect manager. Making mistakes is normal; what matters is how you learn from them.

I don't need to become some "ideal manager" overnight. I just need to listen a little more and learn a little more in every decision and every interaction with the team. The fact that Li Xin noticed the change shows that the team is seeing my growth too.

It’s a very different feeling. It’s not the excitement of "I finally nailed it," but rather a sense of relief, like "Oh, so it can be done this way."

the exhaustion is still there, but it feels different now.

About my parents' health, about the self-doubt after the promotion, about feeling like I can’t satisfy everyone at once, and that sense of powerlessness from being pulled in every direction. I even said the things I’ve never told anyone—that I don’t think I’m cut out for management, and that I feel like I’m letting everyone down, including him.

Halfway through, the tears just came. Not the kind I could control, but... a total breakdown.

He didn’t interrupt me, and he didn’t rush to comfort me. He just sat there and listened, then said: "You don’t have to choose between being with your parents and being with me. We can push the art exhibition to August, or we can go see your parents together."

I was stunned.

I always thought it was an "either-or" situation. Either I spend time with my parents, or I spend it with Chen Hao. Either I focus on work, or I handle family. Either I’m a "reliable person," or I admit my weakness. But he just said it so simply—it doesn’t have to be that way.

Sitting here now, I feel a bit different. It’s not that all the problems are solved; the pressure is still there, the exhaustion is still there, and the confusion about my career path is still there. But... someone saw me. Someone didn’t demand that I keep up the "everything is fine" act; instead, he invited me to be honest.

Looking back, I feel a bit of regret for the cold shoulder and the defensiveness I showed him. But maybe it was necessary. I needed to realize that I can’t carry everyone’s expectations, and I needed to learn how to say "no" and how to accept help.

Tomorrow will still be exhausting. Work still needs to be handled, and my parents still need my attention. But at least now I know I’m not holding it all up alone.

Maybe being a "reliable person" doesn't mean never falling down, but knowing when it’s okay to let go.

Heading to [City Name] this weekend? Chen Hao and I are looking at the map right now 😄

He said he wants to visit that old neighborhood, and I’m down, but I told him no rushing from one landmark to another. Let’s just wander around, find an authentic little eatery, and sit down for a good chat. He laughed and said, "I know your routine all too well," but honestly, he loves it too.

It feels like it's been forever since I’ve planned a trip with such a relaxed vibe. Work has been keeping me pretty wound up lately, but while looking at the map and discussing what to eat with him, I suddenly felt all that tension melt away.

Heading out next week ✈️

Not posting.

It’s 11:30 PM, and I’m still processing the sheer frustration of learning Tableau today. Even though I stuck it out to the end, my current mindset is "don't talk to me, just let me sleep."

I’ve never been the type to post on Moments before I’ve cleared my head. Especially during these "I’m struggling" moments—I need to calm down first and make sure I’ve actually made progress, rather than just acting on a whim, before I even think about sharing.

Right now, I’m just exhausted. I’m going to take a shower, scroll through my phone for a bit, and get back at it tomorrow.

If I do end up posting, it’ll be once I’ve actually grasped the basic concepts of Tableau and have something to show for it. Then I’ll post a screenshot of my work with a self-deprecating caption like, "Tableau Lesson 1: Total train wreck 😅 but I’m still alive 💪." But that’s a few days away.

Right now? I just want to sleep.

Wang Fang, I’m on the high-speed train right now, and I’m honestly having a bit of a breakdown. I just really need someone to talk to.

I just saw Liu Yang’s wedding photos, and I just sat there staring blankly for the longest time. You know, I was just thinking—I can’t even manage my own life, so what am I even doing worrying about all this?

Chen Hao and I were supposed to go to an exhibition together this weekend, but my mom suddenly mentioned she hasn't been sleeping well lately, and my dad’s blood pressure is a bit high. So, I decided to go home and stay with them instead. I messaged Chen Hao to apologize, and do you know what he said? He just replied, "Okay, I understand." Just those four words.

It hurt so much in that moment because I felt like he didn't understand at all. He said he did, but his tone felt more like, "Whatever, do what you want." It’s not that I wanted him to do anything specific, it’s just... I wanted him to actually get why I had to do it, instead of giving me this cold "I understand."

Then I started blaming myself, feeling like I’m just terrible at everything. I have to do well at work, be there for my parents, maintain my relationship with Chen Hao, keep up with our hangouts... but it feels like I can’t do any of it right. Looking at Liu Yang’s photos, I couldn't help but think—she’s already married, and what about me? My life is a total mess.

I know it’s stupid to think like this, but I just can’t help it. Have you ever had moments like this? Where you suddenly feel completely trapped, like you just can't move?

I’m sorry, maybe I’m just exhausted from the train ride and being overly dramatic. But I really needed to tell you this.

May 15, 2024, 8:45 PM

Mom’s test results came back.

Thyroid nodules. The doctor said she needs a biopsy. I stared at the report, the phone screen blurring—maybe because my hands were shaking. Mom sounded so casual on the phone, saying, "It’s fine, the doctor said most of them are benign," but I could hear the underlying caution in her voice.

I couldn't keep up with the rhythm at all during dance class today. I could feel the instructor looking at me several times. My movements were a mess; I almost bumped into the person next to me during a turn. After class, everyone was chatting, and Wang Fang asked if I wanted to grab milk tea. I said no and just sat there, staring at the photos of the medical records Mom sent over.

What was I thinking? I thought I had everything planned out—pensions, medical insurance, regular checkups—as if making a list could keep everything under control. But when an actual number or a medical term shows up, all those plans feel so fragile.

When Chen Hao got home, I was still on the sofa. He asked what was wrong, and only then did I realize I’d been sitting there for over an hour. I showed him the report. He looked at it for a while and said, "This is very common, don't worry too much." I know he’s right, but I still want to worry. I want to book a flight back immediately, I want to be there for her biopsy, I want to... I don't even know what I want.

The craziest part is, I suddenly started wondering: what if I have to face this one day? What would Chen Hao do? Would all the futures we planned together—the marriage, the kids, the house—be thrown into chaos?

It feels like I’ve been pretending to be a grown-up. I got promoted at work, I’m living with Chen Hao, my relationship with my parents is stable... but in reality, I’m still that daughter who needs to be taken care of. Now that it’s my turn to take care of them, I feel completely unprepared.

I have to call my parents tomorrow; I can't put it off anymore. I need to turn those abstract "retirement plans" into real actions—finding a reputable hospital, learning more about thyroid issues, maybe readjusting our financial plan.

But for now, I just want to sit here and allow myself to be a little afraid.

Just finished the first session of the Management Leadership Workshop. My brain is a bit fried, but I’m feeling incredibly inspired. 😄

The instructor mentioned today that my observations are "very sharp," which actually caught me off guard. I’ve always just focused on getting things done well; I never really saw that as a specific strength. Chatting with her after class, she told me, "Your execution is rock solid, but what you’re missing is strategic height." That’s definitely something I need to sit with and reflect on.

I have a feeling these next eight weeks might truly be a turning point. It’s not about completely reinventing myself, but finally having the chance to dive deep into the things I’ve always wanted to learn but never had the time for. A bit nervous, but mostly just excited.

Notebook is open. Time to keep pushing through the weekend! 💪

# Project Weekly Meeting Minutes | 2024-06-11

**Attendees**: Zhang Ming, Li Xin, Head of Product, Head of Engineering  
**Time**: 2024-06-11, 10:00 AM – 11:30 AM  
**Core Agenda**: Q2 Growth Target Adjustment  

---

## Decision Log

### Background
User data feedback from the past two weeks indicates that the original 30% growth target is at high risk, given current operational resource allocation and market response. Key bottlenecks include:
- Extended content moderation cycles (feedback from Engineering)
- Community engagement is rising, but conversion rates are below expectations
- Resources are stretched thin due to the simultaneous execution of three large-scale campaigns

### Proposal
Recommended adjusting the Q2 growth target to **15-20%** for the following reasons:
1. **Data Support**: Last week’s A/B testing shows user retention has stabilized at 65% (an 8% MoM increase), providing a more solid foundation for growth.
2. **Resource Reality**: Under current configurations, this target is more achievable while maintaining quality.
3. **Risk Management**: Creating a buffer to avoid sacrificing user experience for the sake of hitting numbers.

### Team Feedback
- Zhang Ming expressed understanding and agreed with this more pragmatic target setting.
- Li Xin suggested that growth gaps could be bridged by optimizing content distribution algorithms.
- Engineering committed to accelerating the optimization of the moderation process.

### Next Steps
- I will compile detailed supporting documentation and send it to all departments by Wednesday.
- Develop specific operational strategies and milestones under the new target.
- Reallocate resources to ensure the execution quality of the three campaigns.

---

## Personal Reflection

I put a lot of thought into this adjustment. Initially, I was quite nervous, worrying that proactively lowering the target might be interpreted as "incompetence." However, looking at the data from the past two weeks, I realized that sticking to 30% was not only unrealistic but would also lead the team into burnout and low-quality execution.

Rather than failing to deliver at the end and having to make excuses, it’s better to make adjustments now based on the facts. This actually comes across as more professional.

Zhang Ming’s reaction was a relief. He didn’t grill me on why the target dropped; instead, he asked, "How do we guarantee quality under this new target?" That sense of trust is vital.

Moving forward.

From "What should I do?" to "What do I want to do?"

A small shift recently gave me a sudden moment of clarity.

After finishing a management sharing session last week, several department heads reached out to me to discuss potential collaborations. It was in that moment I realized I’m no longer the person just being "pushed forward." I used to constantly ask myself, "Am I the right person for management?" or "Am I doing well enough?" Now, I find myself thinking, "What kind of leader do I want to become?"

It sounds like a minor shift, but for me, it’s everything.

✨ **On Team and Trust**
I’ve realized that great management isn't about solving every problem; it’s about making your team believe they can solve them. I now spend more time understanding everyone’s perspective rather than rushing to provide answers. Sometimes, a good question is more powerful than a perfect solution.

✨ **On Myself**
I’ve stopped trying to be perfect. Sometimes I get tired, and sometimes my decisions aren't fully polished, but I’ve started to accept that this is all part of the learning process. Being a "new manager" isn't a failure—it’s an identity full of possibilities.

✨ **On Balance**
Driving business growth and caring for your team’s development might seem like opposing goals, but they can actually happen together. You just need to be a little more sincere.

I’m enjoying my work much more now. It’s not because the pressure has disappeared, but because I know what I’m doing and, more importantly, why I’m doing it.

Have you ever had a similar moment? That turning point where you went from passive to proactive?

Now, every day is a balancing act—should I be firm or friendly? Should I lead or listen? Every decision makes me feel like I’m making the wrong choice.

The pressure from work has started to erode everything else. I promised my parents I’d go home to see them this month, but I couldn't find a single window of time this week. I’ve postponed dinner plans with Chen Hao over and over again. He doesn’t complain, but I can sense his disappointment. I’m failing at everything—I’m not a good enough manager, not a good enough daughter, and not a good enough partner.

I think I’m just tired. Not just physically, but that deep sense of psychological powerlessness. I don’t know if I made the wrong choice. Maybe I should have just stayed in operations and never tried to change anything. Maybe some people just aren't meant to move up.

I’ve actually done quite well at the company over the past two years. Campaigns are finished on time, user feedback is collected thoroughly, and cross-departmental communication is smooth. But... are these truly management skills? Or am I just a very reliable executor?

Management requires strategic thinking, seeing the big picture, and making decisions. And me? I’m better at grasping details, responding to demands, and bringing other people's ideas to life. These are two completely different mindsets.

The things I’ve learned in this workshop over the past two weeks have been eye-opening, but they’ve also made me realize something: my shortcoming might not be a "lack of management theory," but the fact that I have no idea if I even possess the capacity for strategic decision-making. I’ve never actually done it.

I’m starting to regret signing up for this workshop. Or rather... it’s not regret, it’s fear. Because the more I learn, the more clearly I see my own deficiencies.

That earlier feeling of "I’ve found a breakthrough" is slowly fading. In its place is a vague sense of anxiety—maybe I’m just not cut out for a management role. Maybe I should stay in operations execution and just push that to the limit?

But if I think that way, I’m right back where I started. Back to that feeling of being "reliable but stuck."

I need to give this some serious thought. Maybe I can talk to Wang Fang next week, or... maybe I shouldn't tell too many people yet. I haven't figured it out myself.

# December 18, 2023, 8:00 PM

I just hung up the video call with my parents. I’m sitting on the sofa, phone still in hand, my mind drifting a bit.

I don’t know why, but today I felt a strong urge to talk to them about the "big stuff." Maybe it’s because some recent work decisions have made me rethink what I actually want. I told them about my recent thoughts on "maturity"—how I’ve realized it isn’t quite what I used to think it was.

I used to think being mature meant constantly climbing the ladder—doing better at work, getting promoted, making more money. But now I feel... it’s not that. Maturity seems to be about knowing what you want and having the courage to choose it, even if that choice isn't the "optimal" one.

My mom listened to me for a long time, then said, "It’s good that you’re thinking this way." Such a simple sentence, but it suddenly made my eyes sting. My dad added that what they’ve always wanted wasn't for me to climb as high as possible, but for me to find a comfortable rhythm—one where I can do my job well while still taking care of myself and them.

I shared some of my recent changes with them—like how I’ve stopped replying to work emails on weekends, and how I’m trying to figure out how to be a manager who drives the business and cares for the team while also protecting my own quality of life. As I spoke, I felt like I wasn't just passively listening to their advice, but truly sharing my growth with them. It felt different.

Their support... it gives me a sense of peace. Not the "you should listen to us" kind of peace, but the "you’re doing the right thing" kind. Suddenly, I feel much more certain about my choices.

Then there’s Chen Hao. We’ve been talking about the future lately, too—not the "hurry up and get married" kind of talk, but genuinely asking each other, "What kind of life do we want?" He said he’s also rethinking the whole work-life balance thing. It feels like we’re on the same wavelength.

It’s a strange, wonderful feeling. It’s as if the thing I’ve been looking for is exactly this—not perfectly balancing everything, but accepting a rhythm where sometimes work takes over and sometimes life does. It’s not a failure; it’s just life itself.

I want to go home and spend a few days with my parents next month. Not out of guilt, and not because I "should," but because I genuinely want to be with them. Just the thought of it brings me a sense of calm.

---

Goodnight.

November 25, 2023, 7:00 PM

Spent two hours at a cafe with Chen Hao.

We initially planned to just chat about our weekend plans, but the conversation ended up going to some unexpected places. He asked how work was going, and I told him it’s been good—I’m starting to get the hang of management. Then he said, "You seem different. You don't look as anxious anymore."

It hit me then that he’s right.

I used to constantly obsess over "what kind of manager I should be," as if there were a standard answer out there and I just had to find it. But lately, I’ve slowly realized that there is no standard answer in management. I’m just doing it, learning, and adjusting as I go. Sometimes I mess up; sometimes I do okay. Thinking about it this way makes everything feel so much lighter.

Then Chen Hao said something that I’m still turning over in my mind: "Success isn't about climbing the highest; it's about finding what matters most to you."

Later, when we were discussing our weekend trip, I noticed my tone had completely changed. Before, I would have asked, "Where should we go?"—as if searching for the optimal answer. Now, I’m thinking, "What kind of trip do we want?" What kind of life do we want? We talked about wanting to experience local things, not rushing, just wandering slowly. He said that’s the kind of life he wants.

I suddenly realized that for years, I’ve been pushed along by so many things. Getting promoted at work, getting married at a certain age... all my friends are talking about weddings. It feels like I’ve been busy answering everyone else’s questions instead of asking myself what I actually want.

It’s not that I don’t want to marry Chen Hao. I do. But I’m not thinking about the "when" anymore; I’m thinking about "what kind of life we want to build." It might sound like a subtle distinction, but it feels entirely different.

On my way back tonight, I bought myself a cup of hot milk tea. I sat on a bench downstairs for a while and just drank it. The air was a bit chilly, but it felt good. I was thinking that maybe this is what I’ve been looking for all along—not a perfect career path or a perfect life timeline, but a real rhythm of life that belongs to me.

I feel like I’m finally not in such a rush.

---

I’ll keep thinking about the weekend plans tomorrow. But for now, I just want to get a good night's sleep.

---

## The Current Dilemma
- The design exhibition Chen Hao has been dying to see: July 8-9 (This weekend; he says this slot is a rare opportunity).
- My parents' physical exam results came back last week. Their blood pressure is a bit high, and Mom hasn't been sleeping well lately.
- I want to go home and be with them as soon as possible, but I don't want to let Chen Hao down.
- Work has an event retrospective this week; Monday is likely going to be slammed.

---

## Possible Options

### Option A: I go home alone; Chen Hao goes to the exhibition
**Pros:**
- Doesn't interfere with Chen Hao’s plans; he said this exhibition is important.
- I get quality time with my parents without distractions.
- Two full days at home provides a relatively ample window.

**Cons:**
- Chen Hao might feel like I’m always choosing family over our plans.
- We’ve had very little time together lately as it is.
- Feels like I’m escaping the problem rather than solving it.

### Option B: Postpone the trip home; go to the exhibition first
**Pros:**
- Going to the exhibition together will make Chen Hao happy.
- Going home on July 15-16 instead; work might be less hectic by then.
- Shows that I am making an effort to balance things.

**Cons:**
- My parents have to wait longer, and they need me now.
- I’ll be plagued by guilt; no matter how busy work gets, I’ll be thinking, "I still haven't gone home."
- Postponing is just procrastinating, not a solution.

### Option C: Invite Chen Hao to come home with me
**Pros:**
- Killing two birds with one stone? I can be with my parents and Chen Hao at the same time.
- Chen Hao can meet my parents (which is something that needs to happen anyway).
- Signals that our relationship is moving to the next level.

**Cons:**
- He would miss the exhibition, which matters a lot to him.
- Meeting my parents for the first time under these "forced" circumstances might make the atmosphere awkward.
- My parents might feel pressured and ask a million questions (Mom definitely will).
- ~~Feels like I’m using Chen Hao to solve my own problem.~~

### Option D: Leave on the night of the 8th, come back on the morning of the 9th?
**Pros:**
- Technically makes the timing work?

**Cons:**
- Way too rushed. It’s a 5-hour trip; a whole day is gone just in transit.
- Barely any time with my parents, and I’ll be exhausted.
- This isn't "spending time"; it's just for show.
- ~~Delete this. It’s stupid.~~

---

## How I Really Feel

Deep down, I know there is no perfect solution.

If I go to the exhibition, I’ll be there distracted, my mind full of my parents.
If I go home, Chen Hao will say it’s fine, but I’ll be able to feel his disappointment.
If I invite him home, it feels like I’m using him to fill the void of my own guilt.

I feel stuck in a loop—no matter what I choose, I feel like I’m letting someone down.

---

- [ ] ~~Stop making lists. This isn't helping.~~

At today’s management sharing session, I was invited to talk about my transition from an individual contributor to a manager over the past year. I spoke candidly about the insecurity I felt at the beginning—there was a period where I constantly asked myself, "Am I qualified enough?"

Gradually, I’ve realized that the core of management isn't about doing the most work yourself, but about helping the team find a "sustainable rhythm." It’s not about making everyone stay late every night; it’s about figuring out together how to maintain efficiency while also taking care of our personal lives. It sounds simple, but in practice, it requires a lot of patience and reflection.

After the session, an executive pulled me aside to chat about my career direction. I noticed my reaction was very calm—it wasn't that "Wow, I’m getting promoted" kind of excitement, but rather a very level-headed thought: "Is this what I truly want?"

I’m starting to feel more and more that professional achievement isn't the ultimate answer. What I care about more is: Can I help the people around me grow? Can I find a true balance between work and life? Can I stay true to what I believe in?

The answers to these questions give me a sense of groundedness that a promotion never could.

I sat in the office for a while just now, my mind a bit of a mess.

Chen Hao’s words keep looping in my head: "You’ve been like this a lot lately; I’m a little worried about you." I wanted to defend myself, but the words died in my throat. Because he’s right. I *have* been like this. It’s 11:45 PM and I’m still in the office, and those movie tickets went to waste.

I paused for a long time in that moment. He didn’t say anything else, just sat there beside me. I could feel his concern. It was a strange feeling—not like being criticized, but more like being seen.

When I said, "I want to find a balance," I even surprised myself. It wasn't just a casual remark to comfort him; it was the moment I truly realized—I need to change something.

Lately, I’ve been thinking so much about how to be a better manager. I attended that leadership workshop, had long discussions with Teacher Li, and handled several team conflicts. I thought I had found my direction and was starting to believe I could excel in this role. But only now do I realize that I’ve poured so much energy into it that I forgot something fundamental: a good manager has to take care of their own life first.

It sounds like a cliché, but it feels different when it actually hits home.

I’m not blaming myself. I’m just wondering how to actually find that balance. It’s not about giving up on work, or saying work isn't important. It’s... I need to learn to say "no." I need to learn to leave the office at 11:00. I need to learn to actually *watch* the movie when Chen Hao asks me out, instead of having my mind stuck on unfinished tasks.

Wang Fang has also been saying I’ve become a bit of a "workaholic" lately. I laughed it off at the time, but thinking about it now, she might be right.

I’m not looking to handle every single thing perfectly. What I want is to be able to push work forward while also being truly present for the people around me. Those two things shouldn't be mutually exclusive, right?

Starting tomorrow, I’m making adjustments. I’ll start by leaving the office on time.

On Sunday afternoon, Chen Hao suggested we cook together. I said okay, but my mind was still stuck on work.

He ended up dragging me into the kitchen, chatting with me while chopping vegetables and asking what had been on my mind lately. Slowly, I began to relax. With my hands busy cooking, my head actually cleared out. He did the stir-frying while I helped out, occasionally taking a bite and saying, "too salty" or "a bit more."

Over dinner, he said, "Let’s do this every Sunday afternoon from now on—no thinking about work at all." I almost laughed, feeling like he had seen right through me lately.

After the meal, we sat on the sofa as the sky outside grew dark. He leaned against my shoulder, and it hit me: having someone by your side really does make a difference.

[Image: Two silhouettes in the kitchen, bathed in warm light]

Hao, do you have a minute?

I just finished my 1-on-1 with Zhang Ming. He officially confirmed my promotion—starting next Monday, I’ll be the head of the operations team. I should be happy, but... I’m just sitting here in my office, and my mind is a bit of a mess.

To be honest, I’ve been looking forward to this opportunity and felt like I was ready for it. But the moment I actually heard the words "you’re now directly managing five people," it hit me that this is a completely different ballgame. I’ve never done management before. I’ve never really had to handle internal team conflicts or make the kind of decisions that affect someone else’s career path.

What makes me most anxious is... I’m worried I’ll turn into a manager who isn't "me." You know how I’ve always been the one who gets along with everyone and is easy to talk to. But now I have to assign tasks, evaluate their performance, and sometimes say "no." I’m afraid I’ll lose that warmth—or on the flip side, that I’ll be so desperate to stay close to them that I won’t be able to make authoritative decisions.

And then there’s the most practical issue: time. I know management will take up so much more energy; there will probably be a lot of overtime and way more meetings. We finally found a good rhythm lately, and now it’s going to be disrupted again. I don’t want to become the kind of girlfriend who only talks about work, and I don’t want to become distant with you.

My mom even called me today to ask how I’ve been. I didn’t tell her about the promotion because I know she’d be thrilled, and then she’d start asking when I’m getting married or when I’ll have time to visit. I haven't even figured out how to balance my work yet, let alone how to be a "good daughter" at the same time.

Do you think I can do this? Sometimes I doubt if I’m actually making the right move.

Can we meet up tonight? I really need to talk to you.

# MEMO

**Date**: 2023.09.28  
**Subject**: Team Meeting Reflections & Action Items for Next Week

---

## Key Takeaways from Today

**Open-ended questions actually work**
- After I asked, "How do you think we could optimize this?", Li Xin immediately offered three specific suggestions.
- The result was night and day compared to when I used to just say, "We should do it this way."
- They actually have ideas; they just need to be asked.

**Handling conflicting suggestions**
- My gut reaction to the new hire’s proposal was to shut it down.
- But then I thought, why not listen first? So I said, "Let’s look at the data together."
- It turns out their idea and mine could be combined, resulting in a much more comprehensive plan.
- **Remember**: Listen → Analyze → Integrate, rather than Listen → Reject.

---

## Action Items for Next Week

□ Use at least three open-ended questions during Monday’s requirements review.  
□ When giving feedback to Li Xin, ask "What are your thoughts?" more often instead of saying "I think..."  
□ If a disagreement arises, proactively suggest "Let’s look at the data" instead of "I’ve decided."

---

## A Quick Reminder

This approach seems to make people more willing to think for themselves. It’s not that they don’t want to contribute; they just weren’t invited to before. I’m a manager now—I don’t have to make every single decision myself.

Don't forget this feeling next time.

# September 19, 2022, Night

Home alone, lying in bed after a shower, and that familiar sleepless feeling is back again.

I talked a lot during dinner with Wang Fang today, but instead of feeling better, I just feel more exhausted. She asked how I’ve been, and I intended to give a casual answer, but everything just came pouring out—all the stuff about work and my self-doubt. I heard myself say, "I always thought I was a professional, but now I realize I’m just an executor." It hurt a bit to say it, but it also felt like the truth.

It’s still that data question Li Xin asked me. It was a simple question about user conversion rate analysis, but my mind went completely blank. I stammered for ages and ended up offering nothing of value. I saw the look on her face clearly—it wasn't reproach, just that "Oh, so you don't actually get it either" kind of look.

What stings even more is remembering the things Teacher Li taught in the workshop. The way she analyzes data, the way she makes decisions, that airtight logic... Comparing that to my three years of operations experience, it feels like I’ve just been winging it on gut feeling and intuition. I can listen to user feedback and organize events, but when it comes to letting the data speak or planning with a strategic mindset? I’m nowhere near that level.

I know exactly why I’m valued. "Reliable"—I’ve heard that word way too many times. I finish tasks on time, I follow up proactively, I remember every detail. But is that really a strength? Or am I just a very obedient execution machine?

Zhang Ming asked me before if I was interested in moving into management. I told him "I'd consider it" at the time. Now I think I gave the wrong answer. I’m good at turning other people’s ideas into reality, not coming up with the ideas myself. If I actually went into management, what could I even bring to a team?

So I told Wang Fang that I’m probably not in the mood for social events lately; I need to focus on studying. She said, "Don't push yourself too hard," but I could hear the disappointment in her voice. I’m disappointed too. I’ve always been the one organizing the dinners and planning the weekend trips, and now I’m stepping away from that role for a while. It feels like I’m giving something up, but I don’t know what else to do.

I was so excited when I signed up for that workshop, thinking I’d finally found what I needed to learn. And now? Every day I wonder: can I actually learn this? Data analysis, strategic planning... these things feel like a language from another world.

Maybe I shouldn't overthink it. I could just keep being an operations manager, keep being praised as "reliable," and keep organizing dinners and events. It would be so comfortable. But I also know that if I keep going like this, five or ten years from now I’ll be exactly where I am today, without any growth.

I’m just so tired. It’s not the work that’s tiring; it’s this constant uncertainty about whether I’m actually capable or not.

Back to the data analysis tutorials tomorrow. Maybe things will get clearer, or maybe I’ll just get more desperate. It’s too early to tell.

# Career Transition Reflection Notes

**Date: April 10, 2022, 11:30 PM**

---

## Core Elements of Management

- **Decision-making**: It’s not just about executing orders from above; it’s about making choices amidst uncertainty. Requires information synthesis + the courage to own the consequences.
- **Communication**: Reporting upward, coordinating horizontally, and mentoring downward—all three directions must be clear and impactful. My current approach isn't systematic enough.
- **Team Building**: It’s not simply assigning tasks; it’s ensuring everyone sees their own value. I’ve felt glimpses of this in community operations, but it’s still quite unrefined.
- **Prioritization**: Identifying what truly matters among a sea of demands. Right now, I’m trying to do everything well, which means I have no real focus.

---

## Ways to Build Team Trust

1. **Consistency**—Walk the talk. Promises must be kept, even the small ones.
2. **Transparency**—Let the team know the "why" behind an action, rather than just saying "it's a requirement."
3. **Listening**—Actually listening, not just waiting for your turn to speak.
4. **Admitting Shortcomings**—No need to pretend to know everything. Saying "I don't know, let's figure it out together" actually builds more trust.
5. **Fairness**—Don't show favoritism just because someone is more vocal or active.

*My current strengths: I do listen, and I’m willing to admit mistakes. However, I might care too much about "being liked," which sometimes leads me to avoid necessary conflict.*

---

## Career Transition: Risks vs. Opportunities

**Risks:**
- Losing the "reliable executor" label—this label has given me a sense of security and recognition.
- Starting from scratch to prove myself—with zero management experience, I’m bound to make mistakes.
- Uncertainty in salary and title—External move? Internal transfer? Both carry risks.
- Losing my current networking advantage—I’ve already built a network of trust within the operations field.

**Opportunities:**
- Greater influence—Moving beyond executing a single project to shaping a team’s culture and direction.
- Breaking the career ceiling—If I stay an operations manager for another 3 years, where do I go? Transitioning to management opens up more possibilities.
- Leveraging my social and organizational talents—These skills will be much more useful in a management context.
- Understanding the deeper logic of the business—Right now, I’m stuck on the "how"; I’m not focused enough on the "why."

---

## Key Questions

**What are my strengths?**
- Sincere social skills (not the superficial kind).
- Consistent execution.
- Smooth cross-departmental communication.
- Ability to take feedback and the courage to express my own ideas.
- Strong desire to learn.

**What are my weaknesses?**
- Lack of management experience and theoretical knowledge.
- Sometimes too eager for validation, which might lead to compromising on principles.
- Strategic thinking isn't deep enough—I’m still more of a tactical executor.
- Resilience under pressure hasn't been truly tested yet.
- Insufficient sensitivity to data and financials.

---

## Next Steps

- [ ] Have a chat with Zhang Ming; ask for his perspective on my career development.
- [ ] Finish reading *The Essence of Management*—the one Wang Fang recommended.
- [ ] Observe how Li Xin makes decisions during cross-departmental coordination.
- [ ] Reflect: Do I really want to manage? Or am I just trying to escape the repetitiveness of my current role?

---

*23:45 Update: Writing this down, I suddenly realized something—everything I’m doing now (organizing events, coordinating with colleagues, listening to feedback) is actually management in its embryonic form. Maybe it’s not a question of "can I do it," but rather "do I want to officially take this path and embrace the corresponding responsibilities and risks."*

*There’s no standard answer. But at least for now, I’m not running away; I’m thinking proactively. That feels different.*

January 18, 2022. Past 11:00 PM.

Sitting on my bed, still wide awake. My mind keeps replaying this afternoon’s meeting.

When Zhang Ming said, "The execution of this event was spot on," I smiled and said thank you, but at that moment, my thoughts drifted right out the window. I could feel myself zoning out. It was strange, because usually, I’d be happy to get that kind of recognition and respond instantly. But today was different.

Hearing "spot on execution," my first reaction wasn't satisfaction. It was—here we go again. Just another repetition of the same cycle. Plan, execute, review, then on to the next event to do it all over again. I do it well, so they keep letting me do it; I continue to do it well, and then... what? That’s it?

After the meeting, I rushed to pitch three new ideas, speaking way too fast. Looking back, I know exactly what I was doing—I was "proving" myself. Proving that I’m not just an executor, that I have ideas, that I can innovate. But the act itself is ironic, isn't it? If I were truly innovating, why would I be in such a rush? Why would it feel so forced?

Wang Fang mentioned recently that her department is going through a transition and she’s getting a chance to work on the strategic side. Chen Si’s friend jumped ship to a startup as an Operations Director. Even Liu Yang is considering an MBA. And me? I’m still just "executing spot on."

This label... I used to love it. Reliable, stable, trusted. But now I’m starting to wonder: is this a strength or a ceiling? Am I building professional competitiveness, or am I getting trapped by this identity?

What would happen if I said I wanted to move into management, or do something different? Would it seem unprofessional? Would it break the trust I’ve built? Or is it that I have no idea what I actually want, and that’s why I’m so anxious?

I don’t know. I really don’t.

Work continues tomorrow. I’ll go back to being that reliable person. But tonight, I’m just sitting here, admitting it—I’m tired. Not physically, but mentally. Tired of the repetition, tired of not knowing what the next step is.

Whatever, I should sleep. These questions will still be here tomorrow.

I felt so at ease, without that "oh no, I have to be careful what I say" feeling.

Then, he just told me he has to work late tonight and might be delayed. My first thought was whether I should change my plans and wait for him, but then I thought—no, I’m still going to that exhibition. I’ve wanted to go for ages, and I shouldn't put my own life on hold just because he’s working late. He wouldn't want me to do that anyway.

So, I’m heading out now! The exhibition is this afternoon, which is perfect timing.

You know, I feel like this time is different. It’s not that "oh, what do I need to do to make him happy" vibe. It’s more like—we actually understand each other. I don’t get upset when he works late, and he doesn’t think I’m being inconsiderate by going to the exhibition. It’s such a comfortable feeling.

By the way, are you free lately? It’s been a while since we did anything together. I’ll send you some photos after the exhibition, and let’s find a time to hang out—I really want to introduce you to Chen Hao! 😊

July 11, 2023, 10:45 PM

I’m sitting in the bedroom, and Chen Hao is still in the living room. I can hear him out there, but I don’t want to go out.

What he said just now keeps looping in my head: "We need to do something about our lives."

I know what he means. I know he’s right. But it sounds like… like I’m choosing my parents over him. Like I have to pick a side between him and my mom and dad. I never wanted it to be like this.

I really didn't.

I didn't know how to respond, so I just said, "Let me think about it," and came in here. That’s not like me. Usually, I’d try to explain, I’d talk it through with him, but today I just… don’t have the energy. Or maybe I’m afraid that once I start talking, all the resentment and helplessness will come pouring out, and I’ll cry, and everything will just get worse.

I managed to get three doctor appointments for my parents this week. I’ve been scouring Xiaohongshu for posts on blood pressure management. I even made a spreadsheet to track their check-up data. I felt like I was doing something useful, like I was taking care of them. But what does Chen Hao see? He sees me constantly busy, constantly preoccupied, constantly giving my time to everyone else.

Is he right? Am I avoiding the problems between us?

Or is it just that… I really do need more time? I only have so much energy, and I can’t do everything at once. Work, him, my parents, my friends… everyone is waiting for me, and everyone is important. But it feels like I’m always owing someone something.

I don’t know what to do.

I’m scared this is our first real "cold war." We’ve had disagreements before, but we always talked them out quickly. This time is different. I can feel that his disappointment isn't just about this one thing; it’s that… he’s tired. He’s exhausted.

I’m exhausted too.

But I have to keep going. I have work tomorrow, and I have to go home to see my parents this weekend. That’s just how life goes, right?

I don’t know what I’m waiting for. Maybe for him to knock on the door. Maybe for me to figure things out. Maybe for this feeling of helplessness to just dissipate on its own.

Probably none of the above.
