September 30, 2023, Night

I just hung up the phone with Dad. Now I’m sitting on the sofa, staring into space a little.

During our video call today, I started rambling about work again. To be honest, this past month has been exhausting. I keep feeling like I’m not doing enough, constantly agonizing over whether I’m actually cut out for a management role. As I talked, my eyes started to sting.

Dad listened until I finished, stayed silent for a moment, and then said, "You know, seeing you grow like this makes me so proud. You aren't running away from difficulties; you are facing them, learning from them, and rising above them."

I didn’t know how to react at first.

Then I told him that I realized something this month—I don’t need to change who I am; I just need to grow from where I stand. As soon as the words left my mouth, I was a bit surprised myself, because that really was the conclusion I’d reached after days of overthinking.

Dad smiled and said, "Now, that is wisdom."

It was such a simple sentence, but it made my eyes well up.

We talked for a while longer. When Dad said, "We’re both learning together," that was the moment I really lost it. I suddenly realized that I’m no longer just the daughter asking her father for answers. We are now like two adults, both fumbling through our own lives, offering each other understanding and support.

It’s a strange, wonderful feeling.

I used to feel like I had to be perfect, that I had to have all the answers, especially at work. But after the setbacks I faced this month, it feels like I’ve finally let go of something. Not my responsibilities, but that obsession with "having to be perfect."

I’m starting to feel that making mistakes isn't actually that scary. What matters is how I learn from them. My team probably isn't as harsh as I imagined, either. What they value is likely just seeing that I’m genuinely trying to improve.

Sitting here now, I feel a sense of relief I’ve never felt before. It’s not that the pressure has completely vanished, but it’s no longer the kind of pressure that feels like despair. It’s turned into… motivation? Or rather, a sense of "I am learning."

Work continues tomorrow, and there will still be challenges. But I feel like I can face them with a different mindset now.

Thank you, Dad. And thank you to this past month for giving me the time to reflect like this.

October 12th, 2:30 PM

I’ve been sitting in my office for over an hour now, doing absolutely nothing but spinning my wheels.

This morning, I heard about the disagreement between Li Xin and the new intern regarding the project. My immediate reaction was to say, "I’ll handle it," but the moment the words left my mouth, I started to regret them. Now, it’s all I can think about—it’s like I’m stuck on something.

I keep playing out the options in my head. Should I listen to their individual perspectives first? Or just hand down a solution? If I lean toward Li Xin, will the intern think I’m being unfair? But if I back the intern, will Li Xin feel like I’m dismissing her experience? I even thought that maybe I should just let them work it out themselves... but would that make it look like I’m dodging my responsibilities?

Every choice feels flawed. I can see the pitfalls behind every single decision.

The scariest part is realizing that I’m afraid—afraid that no matter what I do, someone is going to get hurt. I’m afraid they’ll say behind my back, "See, she’s just a new manager; she doesn't know how to make a call." I’m afraid Zhang Ming will think I’m being unprofessional.

So, in the end, I did nothing. I told myself, "Just cool off for a bit, you'll figure it out this afternoon." But that decision has only made me more anxious. Now I feel like I’m sitting on a time bomb, just waiting for it to go off.

I’m starting to wonder if I’m even cut out for management. It was so much more comfortable back when I was just an individual contributor—I’d do the work well, get recognized, and move on to the next task. There weren't all these feelings to consider, all these trade-offs. Now, every decision takes forever because I’m terrified of stepping on someone’s toes.

But then again, if I back down now and give up on this opportunity, wouldn't all my previous hard work be for nothing?

There’s a message from Chen Hao on my phone asking when I’ll be off work. I glanced at it but didn't reply. I know he’s been waiting for me to spend more time with him lately, but my brain just doesn't have room for anything else right now. I don’t even have the energy to feel guilty. I’m just... exhausted.

I need to figure out how to handle this. But right now, I can’t think straight at all.

# November 7, 2022, 7:00 PM

I’m sitting in the office. Everyone else has left, but I’m still here. It’s not because of any urgent work; I just... need a moment to myself.

What happened in the workshop earlier keeps replaying in my head. When Mr. Li finished explaining the final project requirements, I felt my chest tighten instantly. I actually took the initiative to walk up to him and say it out loud: "I’m worried my data analysis isn't deep enough yet." I could feel myself trembling as I spoke. It was a bit embarrassing, but also... a relief?

The old me would have probably stayed silent or gone home and overanalyzed it in my head. But not today. I faced it head-on.

And then, what Mr. Li said keeps echoing in my ears: "You already have a solid foundation in execution; now you need to learn to support your decisions with data and systems thinking."

I’ve always seen "reliable" as my label, and sometimes I’ve been quite proud of it. But hearing him say that made me realize... that’s not the finish line. It’s just the beginning. All the things I’ve been doing, the work I’ve been recognized for—they’re just the foundation. If I want to go further, if I really want to manage and make decisions, I can’t just stay at the "reliable execution" stage.

It’s a bit scary, but more than that... it’s the feeling of being seen. It’s as if he saw right through my anxiety, but instead of dismissing me, he was telling me—you can do better.

I drew a big ★ in my notebook and marked the key points of the project. I pressed my finger against the paper for a long time. It felt a little silly, but I needed to do it. This isn't just a course assignment. What is it then... it’s me proving something, I guess. Proving that I’m not just a reliable person—that I can still learn, still improve, and still think about problems in a completely different way.

Lately, from the moment I signed up for the workshop until now, I’ve gone through so much self-doubt. The anxiety, the fear that I wouldn't be able to learn it, the worry that I was wasting my time. But now... now I can feel the joy that comes from learning itself. That "oh, so that's how it works" feeling, that "I can do this" feeling.

I always thought I knew myself well, but it turns out I’ve been underestimating my own ability to learn.

Tomorrow, I keep going. ★ I have to do this project well. Not to prove it to anyone else, but... to prove it to myself.

February 13, 2022. Late night, 00:47

I’ve been lying here for a long time, but I still can’t fall asleep.

I was scrolling through my phone in bed just now and somehow stumbled upon a post titled "The Career Dilemma of a Third-Year Operations Professional." I stared at that headline for five minutes. My heart was racing a bit, and I couldn't really say why. Maybe because that’s me.

Then I started searching for stuff about career transitions. For a whole hour. I kept reading other people's stories—some moved into management, some jumped to big tech firms, and some just started their own businesses. I sat there in the dark looking at these options as if I were watching someone else's life.

My mind kept drifting back to Wang Fang’s rotation plan. She’s moving into project management; said she wanted to try something different. I’m happy for her, I really am. But at the same time, I can't help but think... what about me? What am I doing?

"The reliable one." That label has followed me for three years. At first, I saw it as recognition, but now... I don't know. Sometimes it feels like a comfortable cage. I’m good at getting things done, good at coordinating, good at keeping everyone happy. But it feels like I’ve just been doing the same things over and over. Planning, execution, review. Repeat. Repeat.

Am I just standing still?

I should have felt excited seeing those options. Management sounds challenging, jumping ship would mean seeing new things, and starting a business... forget it, that’s too far-fetched. But I didn’t feel excited; instead, I felt a bit... powerless? It’s like all those options are behind a layer of something. I know I *could* do them, but I don't know if I *want* to.

That’s the problem, isn't it?

I like my job, I really do. I like planning events with my colleagues, I like seeing user feedback, and I like the feeling of being needed. But "liking it" and "wanting to do it forever" seem to be two different things.

Late-night thoughts always magnify everything. I’ll probably be fine when I wake up tomorrow. But right now, I’m just wondering—is being "reliable" protecting me, or is it holding me back?

I haven't figured it out yet.

Maybe I'll have it figured out after some sleep.

(But I know I won't be falling asleep anytime soon.)

Lately, I’ve been reflecting on a question: What makes a "good manager"?

I used to think it meant making the right call every single time and never making mistakes. But that mindset just led to constant second-guessing before every decision, paralyzed by the fear of getting it wrong. Then someone told me that making mistakes is actually a normal part of management. What matters isn't being flawless, but how you learn from those mistakes.

It sounds like typical "chicken soup for the soul," but the more I think about it, the more it rings true. I’ve spent so much time obsessing over whether my approach is right, when in reality, I’ve just been chasing a "perfection" that doesn't exist.

I’ve been reading a book lately that says, "The capacity for continuous learning is more important than initial perfection." That really hit home for me.

One step at a time. I need to allow myself to make mistakes and be imperfect. Ironically, that’s the only way to go the distance.

💭

# 📅 Work Priority Adjustment - Q4

**Date**: Dec 2, 2023 - Dec 31, 2023  
**Time**: All Day  
**Category**: Work | Priority  
**Reminder**: None

---

## 📌 Notes

Declined the Marketing Department's invitation for a cross-departmental project. Reason: Several ongoing community operation campaigns have entered the final sprint, and the year-end user satisfaction survey still needs to be completed. I’m spread too thin; rather than half-heartedly participating, it’s better to focus on doing my current tasks well.

I also included some suggestions in the email—they could reach out to Li Xin or other colleagues—and shared a few of my thoughts. Zhang Ming said "good call" after reading the email, which means I didn't step on any toes this time.

**Key Reminder**: Maintain this boundary. Don't backtrack just because you feel bad or want to be helpful. Limited energy must be spent on what matters most.

---

## 💭 Why record this?

In the past, I might have automatically said "yes" just because I was invited, only to end up anxious about the workload later. Now I’m learning to say "no" and provide solid reasoning. This isn't about rejecting others; it’s about being responsible for my own work.

A manager needs to know how to make choices. Choosing means letting go.

Heading to Suzhou with Chen Hao next month! He mentioned wanting to check out the local craft workshops, so we might try our hand at pottery or something similar. When I brought up the plan during a video call with my parents earlier, my mom said, "Seeing you like this makes us feel so much at ease." 😊

Sometimes I feel like life is just about slowly finding that rhythm of walking alongside the right person. It’s nothing grand or dramatic—it’s just that I want to go where he wants to go, and he’s happy to try the things I want to try. It’s actually quite rare to find someone whose pace matches yours so well.

Time to start doing some homework! I’m looking for a few interesting little shops. If you’ve been to Suzhou and have any recommendations, please let me know!~

Wang Fang, sorry for acting a bit off during dinner just now 😔 I wasn’t mad at you, I just... suddenly felt a bit drained, you know?

First of all, I want to say I’m truly happy for you! Rotating to a new department is a great thing, and I mean it. I’m sorry if my own mood dampened the moment earlier.

To be honest, I’ve been thinking about it ever since you mentioned this opportunity in our chats. I couldn't help but wonder... why haven't I had those kinds of thoughts? It’s not jealousy, it’s just a sudden realization that I’ve been stuck in the same spot. Every day is more of the same—user feedback, campaign planning, cross-department coordination... I do a good job and people recognize my work, but I guess that’s exactly the problem 😅

I probably need some time to figure out what I actually want. But I wanted to ask, how did you decide to go for this opportunity? Was it a sudden spark, or had you been observing and preparing for a while? I feel like I’m lacking that drive to "proactively strike."

Anyway, let’s talk more another day? I think I need some time to process things.

Chen Hao, do you have a minute? I wanted to chat with you about something.

I just got off the phone with my parents, and they said I seem to be in much better spirits lately. I’ve noticed it myself too—it’s like... I’m not so wound up anymore?

I’ve been thinking about how I’ve been adjusting things over the past six months. I’ve found a better rhythm at work and stopped feeling like I have to handle everything perfectly. And I’ve made a decision—starting next month, I’m going to set aside one weekend every month to go home and see my parents. Just for a day, but I’m making it a fixed thing.

You know, I used to be constantly torn between being "busy" and feeling "guilty." I felt like I was too busy with work to spend time with them, but then I’d feel like I should be doing more. I realize now that it’s not about choosing between the two; it’s about being proactive with planning so that there’s room for both.

It made me wonder if we should think about things this way too—not just being pushed along by expectations, but actually sitting down and asking ourselves: what kind of life do we really want?

I’m not saying things have to be a certain way, it’s just... I feel like I’ve found some clarity lately and wanted to share that with you. I’d love to hear what’s been on your mind lately.

# October 25, 2023, Evening

Sitting in the office, it’s already dark outside. Everyone else has left, and I’m still here—not because of a mountain of work, but because I just need to be alone for a while.

The conversation I had with Zhang Ming earlier keeps playing on a loop in my head.

He said, "Your thinking is very clear right now. This is exactly the kind of vision a good manager should have."

Just that one sentence. I don’t know why it hit me so hard.

I’ve been asking myself lately—am I really cut out for management? Ever since that conflict within the team, I’ve started questioning every decision I make. I’ve been thinking that maybe I should just go back to the execution level. It was so much simpler then. I know how to run a project and how to collaborate with colleagues, but management? Navigating the complexities of human relationships? I’m really not sure.

So when Zhang Ming said that, it felt like being pulled up. Not like being pulled out of water, but more like a confirmation: "You’re headed in the right direction."

But that makes me even more confused.

Why do I need validation this badly? It’s not like me. I’ve always felt I was fine, that I could handle most things and didn't rely much on others' opinions. But now, a single sentence can change my mood for the entire afternoon. What does that say about me? That I’m actually fragile? That my uncertainty about this new identity is deeper than I care to admit?

My shoulders have finally relaxed. My pace of speech has slowed down. This feeling is real; I’m not faking it.

Still, I wonder how long this sense of reassurance will last. The next time I hit a snag, will I spiral back into self-doubt?

What do I need to do to truly validate this path? Not by relying on someone else’s words, but through my own actions, experiences, and learning. Maybe I need to look into more case studies to see how other managers handle similar situations. Maybe I need to talk more with Chen Hao and Wang Fang—actually listen to their thoughts, rather than only reaching out to them when I’m on the verge of a breakdown.

Also, I haven’t really been there for them lately. Work has consumed so much of my time and energy that I haven't even sat down to have a proper talk with Chen Hao.

More tomorrow. But at least for today, I know I’m not completely off track.

Maybe that’s enough.

# 2023.03.11

Sitting in the office. Li Xin just left my desk, and I’m still pretending to check my emails. In reality, my head is completely filled with thoughts of tomorrow’s photography exhibition.

After dinner with Wang Fang yesterday, I didn't sleep well all night. It wasn't insomnia exactly; I was just lying in bed asking myself the same question over and over—is this really what I want? Or is it just the novelty of it all?

But every time I got to that point, the answer was the same: No, this is real.

I still remember clearly what Chen Hao said. He said, "I think many people have it backwards. They think design is about changing the world, but actually, design is about understanding the world." Right then, I thought of that book *Nonviolent Communication*. I’d discussed it with Wang Fang before—how to truly hear another person. Design and communication are actually the same thing; they are both about trying to understand someone’s real needs, rather than imposing your own ideas.

It turns out he’s also reading *The Psychology of Everyday Things*. We talked for half an hour about the relationship between "usability" and "humanity." His way of thinking is very clear, but not cold. Some people come across as arrogant when they talk logic, but he isn't like that. He pauses to ask for my thoughts, and he actually listens instead of just waiting for his turn to speak.

It reminded me of why I hate those forced blind dates so much. It’s not the dates themselves I hate, but that "completing a task" feeling—as if I must find someone before I turn 27, and then everything will be solved. My mom occasionally mentions, "Look, your classmates are all married," and my dad says, "Don't listen to your mother, let nature take its course," but that invisible pressure is still there.

Yet, when I was chatting with Chen Hao, I didn't feel any pressure at all. I just wanted to keep listening to him, to know how he views things. When he asked why I like attending all those different workshops, I naturally started talking about pottery and dance, and how I feel life should be a process of constantly trying new things. He didn't think I was flighty; instead, he said, "That’s cool. You’re using action to understand the world."

Then he asked, "Are you free this weekend? A friend of mine is hosting a photography exhibition. I think you might like it."

I said yes without a moment's hesitation.

Thinking back now, that "lack of hesitation" is important. I didn't calculate in my head whether I should "avoid appearing too eager," I didn't wait for him to ask again, and I didn't say "let me check and get back to you." It was just natural—I wanted to go, so I agreed.

At lunchtime, I caught myself smirking in the mirror, and Li Xin saw me. She said, "You've been very happy lately." I said, "Really?" She said, "Yes, even the look in your eyes is different."

I didn't tell her about Chen Hao. It’s not time yet. But I think after I get back this weekend, I might tell Wang Fang. She’ll be thrilled, she’ll grill me for every detail, and then she’ll say, "I knew you two would be a good match."

Two more hours until I can leave work. I need to focus and finish this afternoon’s work report.

But I’m really looking forward to tomorrow.

Sometimes we think being "reliable" means having to be omnipotent.

But in truth, being honest about your limitations is a form of respect toward others.

I’ve only recently realized that saying "I can't" requires far more courage than forcing yourself to say "I can."

— Good night.

# Saturday, June 8, 2024 – Sunny

Yesterday’s video call with my parents has been playing on a loop in my head.

I had intended to just chat about work and life like usual, but I suddenly blurted out everything I’ve been thinking about lately—their health, retirement plans, whether they should move in with me... I spoke so fast, and many of my thoughts were probably disorganized. I could feel my own anxiety; it was like I was checking off a list, trying to use "planning" as a way to dissolve that sense of powerlessness.

After I finished, my dad paused for a long time. I thought he was going to start telling me not to worry so much or something like that. Instead, he said, "Xiaoyu, don't sacrifice your own growth for our sake."

I was stunned for a moment.

He went on to say that he and Mom are still in decent health, and what matters most to them is seeing me live well. If I were to sacrifice my own opportunities just to take care of them, that would actually be what worries them the most. He spoke calmly, but I could feel the weight behind his words.

After hanging up, I sat there for a long time.

I realized what I’ve been doing these past few days—I’ve been using "planning for them" to soothe my own anxiety. I had interpreted "family responsibility" as "inevitable sacrifice," as if that were the only way to prove I’m a filial daughter. But Dad was actually telling me that a mature family relationship isn't a one-way street of giving; it’s about mutual support.

What they need isn't for me to give up my development to stay by their side, but for me to keep growing and then move forward together with them within my means.

The shift is subtle, but it feels significant.

I’ve started reorganizing my thoughts from these past few days. Retirement planning, regular check-ups, financial support—these are all still necessary, but they shouldn't stem from anxiety and guilt. They should come from rational arrangements and genuine care. It’s like what I do with Wang Hao at work; I’m helping others grow while also fulfilling my own role. It’s not a sacrifice; it’s mutual empowerment.

When I told Chen Hao about this last night, he said, "You’re exactly right to think that way. Being able to take care of yourself is the best way to repay your parents." He also said that once we have more resources in the future, we can do more for them together. It’s not about "if I give something up," but "what can we do together when we both become better versions of ourselves."

This feeling is truly different.

My career is moving forward, my relationship with Chen Hao is moving forward, and I have a clearer vision for my parents' retirement... These things are no longer competing with one another; they are supporting each other within a larger framework. I don’t need to switch identities between them. I am simply living a complete, multi-dimensional life.

I’m a bit tired, but I feel fulfilled. And very grateful.

Grateful for what my dad said. Sometimes, a single sentence from your parents can change your entire understanding of life.

It’s not about how impressive the performance numbers are, or how high I climb the corporate ladder. It’s about this—have I helped the people around me see their own potential?

Talking with a few team members today about their career dilemmas and the directions they want to explore, I realized that more than telling them "what to do," I want to be there to help them figure out "what you truly want."

Sometimes I think that if I were to leave this position one day, I wouldn’t want people to remember me for the number of projects I completed. Instead, I’d hope they say, "Working with Xiaoyu helped me gain a much clearer understanding of myself."

Maybe it sounds a bit idealistic 😄 but I feel like this is what I truly care about right now.

I’ve also been reflecting on my own pace—it’s not about making everything perfect, but about finding a sustainable way to work, to mentor, and to grow. I’ve recently discovered that when I stopped chasing a "perfect balance," I actually found a real one.

In the new year, I’ll keep moving forward at this steady pace 🌱

March 10, 2023, Night

Sitting on the sofa, my phone is still open to the chat with Chen Hao. I’ve re-read it several times now—it’s a bit silly.

Wang Fang said I was spacing out during the entire dinner today and asked if I was just exhausted from work. Ha, how could I admit that I was actually thinking about something someone said?

He said, "So many people are busy checking off life milestones that they forget to ask themselves what they actually want."

I froze the moment he said it. It felt like he was talking about me. For the past few months, I’ve been thinking about my career, but more often than that, I’ve been thinking—I’m 27, everyone around me is getting married, my parents are starting to ask questions, and my WeChat moments are flooded with wedding invitations. It feels like I’m being pushed along by an invisible timetable. But no one has ever asked me what *I* want.

And then Chen Hao asked.

We talked about everything from product design to life planning. He said that what users need most isn't to be force-fed content, but to be seen. To be understood. We talked for a long time, and I realized our rhythms are so similar—neither of us is the type who needs an answer right away, but we’re both thinking deeply.

On the way home, I couldn't stop thinking about it. This feeling is so unfamiliar. The feeling of being truly understood.

It wasn't the "you should do this" kind of advice, nor was it the "you're so impressive" kind of praise. It was just... he was actually listening to what I was saying, and then letting his ideas collide with mine. We were speaking the same language.

I don't know what this means. It might mean nothing, or it might mean everything. But at least for now, I no longer feel like entering the "marriage market" is something I'm being forced into. I’m making a choice, not completing a task.

It feels really good.

Should I reach out to him tomorrow? Or wait and see? Never mind, I won't think about that yet. I’ll just let this feeling linger a little longer.

Wang Fang, I wanted to tell you something.

Chen Hao just had a talk with me. Remember that late-night call I made to you? Everything I said then... he actually took it all in.

I thought he’d be angry or keep giving me the cold shoulder. But he didn't. He just said, very directly, "I know you're hiding something. I don't want to guess; I want to hear it from you." And then I just... broke down. I told him everything. My parents, the pressure at work, how exhausted I am, how much I’ve been doubting myself... all of it.

The most surprising part was that he didn't demand I make a choice. He said he could postpone the exhibition, or we could just go see my parents together. He told me, "We’ll figure this out together; you don't have to carry it all alone."

I was in tears listening to him. I’ve always felt like I couldn't please everyone, like I was letting everyone down. But after he heard everything, he just said, "You don't need to be perfect. You just need to be honest."

I’m still a bit dazed. It feels like that weight that’s been crushing my chest these past few days has suddenly lightened. It’s not completely gone, but... I can breathe.

I’m exhausted, but I don't feel quite so hopeless anymore.

Are you asleep?

# March 10, 2022 Late Night

Insomnia again.

What Li Xin said this afternoon keeps looping in my head. I proposed a new event plan, and after listening, she just said, "Isn't this the same old stuff? Just a different skin." I wanted to push back right then, but the words got stuck in my throat because I knew she was right.

I sat there in silence for a few seconds, feeling my face heat up. It wasn't anger; it was the sting of being seen. It’s that feeling when someone points at you and says, "Look, you’re just repeating yourself," and you have no way to deny it.

I’ve always told myself I’m doing creative work—planning, innovating. But thinking back on the past two years, what I’ve been doing really is just a cycle: user campaigns, community operations, data reviews, and then the same process all over again the next month. Change the theme, change the copy, change the timing, but the bones are exactly the same.

I posted that "Another week" update on WeChat Moments, mostly to express this sense of exhaustion. But I didn't dare be too blunt. When Wang Fang asked if I was tired lately, I just said work’s been busy and changed the subject. Honestly, I don’t even know how to explain it.

The scariest part is realizing I’ve grown used to this repetition. Every day I come into the office, I’m still that enthusiastic person who takes the lead on organizing things. My colleagues still think I’m dependable; Zhang Ming is still praising my "strong execution." But I know that this "dependability" is becoming a comfortable trap.

I wonder if I should move into management? Or jump ship? Or do something entirely different? But the moment I think about breaking this identity I’ve built—the one everyone recognizes—I get scared. Scared of losing the "reliable" label, and scared that maybe I’m not actually that good.

Liu Yang got promoted recently, and Chen Si’s friend is raising seed money for a startup... It’s not jealousy, really. It’s just this feeling that everyone is moving forward while I’m running in place—I’m just doing it very steadily.

Am I overthinking? Or do I really need to make a change?

I don't know.

It’s 2:00 AM, and I have an early meeting tomorrow. But I think I’m going to lie here a little longer.

# March 25, 2023, Night

Just got back from dinner. Sitting on my bed after a shower, my head is still a bit of a mess.

Chen Hao said something today that I’ve been chewing on ever since: "You always make everyone else feel comfortable." Wang Fang was there too and nodded in agreement. I just laughed it off and said, "No, I don't," but in that moment, something flickered in my heart.

It feels good to be seen. The fact that he noticed those details—how I mind people’s feelings, how I keep the conversation flowing—makes me feel... understood? That’s one of the reasons I like him. But why does it also make me feel a little anxious?

I’m wondering: do I really "always make others comfortable," or have I just developed a habit of automatically switching into this mode whenever I’m around people? I do it at work, I do it on social media, and now, it seems I’m unconsciously doing the same thing with Chen Hao.

It’s not a bad thing. I like this version of myself, and I like seeing people relax because of my efforts. But...

But I need to make sure I don’t change too much for him.

When this thought popped up, it actually scared me a little. We haven’t even been together that long; why am I thinking about this already? Am I being too sensitive? Or is this a normal warning sign, and I should be listening to my inner voice?

I like Chen Hao. I really do. He seems to understand me and supports me in trying new things. But I suddenly realized that I haven’t been "uncomfortable" around him yet. I haven't been willful, or stubborn, or said "no" to him. I’ve just been that person who "makes others feel comfortable."

Will there come a day when I want to be left alone and he thinks I’ve changed? If I insist on my own way, will he think I’m not so "easygoing" anymore?

I’m not doubting this relationship. I’m really not. I just... need to remind myself that no matter how much I like someone, I still have to leave space for myself. I have to protect the parts of me that belong only to me.

My mom said something once that I didn't pay much attention to at the time: "Good girls are the most likely to lose themselves in a relationship." I was single back then and thought she was just being a worrywart. Now... I think I understand.

I’ll think about it more tomorrow. I’m a bit tired now. But I need to write this thought down. I can't forget it.

# Project Retrospective & Career Reflections | 2022.06.28

**Project Timeline**: Mid-to-late June – Cross-departmental user campaign planning and execution

---

## Execution Summary

This week, I saw the Community 618 campaign through from initial planning to final launch. The project involved Product, Tech, Marketing, and Customer Service, requiring coordination across roughly 15 meetings. The final data looks solid—new user retention reached 115% of our target. I’m a bit surprised by this result, but mostly relieved.

## Key Takeaways

**1. Getting on the "same page" is critical**

In the past, I often hit a wall with the Product team when it came to requirement alignment. This time, I changed my approach—instead of just handing over requirements, I started by understanding their technical constraints and priorities before proposing a solution. At one point, a PM told me, "You have a much deeper understanding of this than before." It made me realize that much of my previous "inefficient communication" was simply because I hadn't truly stepped into their shoes.

**2. Balancing "speed" and "stability" in decision-making**

Two days before the launch, a bug was reported that could potentially impact user experience. I was torn over whether to delay the rollout. I ended up doing a quick impact assessment—identifying which users would be affected, the severity of the impact, and the cost of the fix—and then discussed it with my lead. We decided on a conditional launch backed by a contingency plan. This process taught me that "stability" isn't about being bug-free; it's about having a clear awareness of risks and a plan to handle them.

**3. Trust is the most expensive currency in team coordination**

This project required collaborating with several departments. What stood out most was working with the Customer Service (CS) team. They needed the campaign rules in advance to prepare FAQs and scripts. I took the initiative to share every version of the plan with them a week early, even before they were finalized. Although this meant more frequent updates, their final understanding of the campaign exceeded my expectations. The CS lead later told me that this level of transparency allowed them to be more proactive with optimization suggestions. It made me realize that being "reliable" isn't just about doing your own job well; it's about enabling your partners to do theirs well, too.

---

## Personal Reflections

To be honest, this project revealed some things about myself that weren't as clear before.

I’ve always considered myself strong in execution and cross-departmental communication, and this project confirmed that. However, I also realized that I still hesitate when faced with complex decisions. For instance, with that bug, I spent way too much time spiraling over "what if" scenarios. It wasn't until my lead asked, "What do you think is the absolute worst-case scenario for this decision?" that I realized I was magnifying the risk rather than assessing it.

Another realization: I’m very comfortable with my current workflow now, and I’m performing well—but this "wellness" is rooted in the execution layer. To move to the next level, I need to do more than just "get things done." I need to think about the "why" and how to "empower others to do well." I consciously tried to do this during this project, but it still feels a bit clunky.

---

## Next Steps

Lately, I’ve been wondering if I should study management more systematically. Not necessarily to jump into a management role immediately, but to understand the underlying logic—how to make decisions amidst uncertainty, how to build team trust, and how to translate individual execution into collective team capability.

I’ve signed up for a management workshop starting in July. I hope to find some answers there and see if I’m genuinely interested in this direction, or if I’m just looking for a change because I’m feeling a bit anxious.

---

Sitting on the sofa; Chen Hao is already asleep. The night view outside the window is quiet, but I’m still not feeling sleepy.

Zhang Ming talked to me today about that new project. To be honest, my heart skipped a beat as I listened to him describe it. The scale... it’s larger than any project I’ve ever led. It spans three business units, and the user base involved is on a completely different level. He said the company has high hopes for me and believes I have the capability.

I didn’t turn him down on the spot, nor did I force myself to say "no problem." I was just very honest with him—I told him this is beyond my current scope of experience and that I need time to think. He actually seemed more relaxed after that, saying he just wanted to hear my genuine thoughts.

Now, lying here, I keep asking myself—am I really ready?

All these years, what I’ve been doing is getting things done and earning people's trust. From assistant to manager, from execution to planning, I’ve taken it step by step. But all of that was within a relatively controllable range. This project is different. If I take it, it means I’ll truly be leading a large team and making decisions I’ve never made before.

But actually... I don’t seem that scared?

It’s strange. If it were me two years ago, I probably would have been anxious for days after hearing about an opportunity like this. Now, it’s more of a... clarity? I know where my shortcomings are, and I know what I can do. The things I learned in the Mid-Level Manager Development Program seem to have been for this exact moment.

I remember when Wang Hao got promoted. Back then, I was thinking about what kind of manager matters most to me. I wanted it to be someone who helps the team grow, rather than someone just focused on climbing the ladder. Now that the opportunity is right in front of me, the question I have to ask is—can I become that person? What does this project mean to me?

Chen Hao asked me before why I’m still overthinking my career development. He said, "You’re already very successful." I was too embarrassed to explain at the time that it’s not about overthinking; it’s about... wanting to find a sustainable rhythm. It’s not to prove anything, but because I really want to know how far I can go.

I need to talk to Zhang Ming again tomorrow. Ask him about his expectations for the project and what resources will be there to support me. Not to make excuses, but to really get a clear picture. If I take this on, I need to know exactly what I’m taking on.

Mom said recently that I seem much more relaxed now, compared to that old "everything must be perfect" tension. I think she’s right. It feels like I no longer need to prove myself through perfection.

So, what about this project? I want to take it. But I want to take it with a clear head, not because I’m being swept up by expectations.

Anyway, time to sleep. I’ll think more tomorrow.

June 5, 2024, Evening

I was at home today. Chen Hao was in the kitchen cooking while I was sitting on the sofa, and suddenly, I just started crying.

It wasn't over any one big thing; it was just work lately, stuff with my parents, and... well, I guess I’m just tired. I’m happy about Wang Hao’s promotion, but it also means I have to take on more. When I video-called my parents last week, Mom mentioned Dad’s blood pressure has been high again, and I started thinking that we really need to put eldercare on the agenda. Then there’s adjusting to living with Chen Hao, thinking about the long-term future... sometimes it just feels like everything is happening at once, and I have to carry it all by myself.

Chen Hao heard me and came out, asking what was wrong. I was going to say, "Nothing, just a bit tired," but this time, I didn't. I just... confessed. I told him I’ve been trying so hard to act strong, like I have everything under control. Being the reliable one at work, taking care of my parents, and being this independent woman with him. It’s exhausting.

He didn't give me any cliché pep talks; he just sat down and listened. Then he said, "You don't have to carry this alone. We can figure it out together." He suggested some very practical things—like scheduling regular video calls with my parents to discuss eldercare plans where he can help organize the information; being a sounding board for my work thoughts whenever I need it; and really sitting down to talk through the pace of our life together.

That was it. Nothing extraordinary, but I suddenly felt this wave of relief.

I always thought being "strong" meant never showing vulnerability to the people I care about. But in this moment with Chen Hao, I realized that true strength might be allowing yourself to be vulnerable—allowing someone else to see the real you. It’s not because I’ve become weaker; it’s because I no longer have to carry everything on my own.

He’s still in the kitchen now, and I can hear him humming. I feel much better.

# Thursday, June 22, 2023 | Sunny

2:20 PM. Sitting in the corner of the office. Li Xin just walked away.

I’m still processing that moment.

"How much of my time will this project take up?" I heard myself ask her. The moment the words left my mouth, I froze. The look in Li Xin’s eyes changed—that "are you backing down?" vibe was unmistakable. She didn't say much, just gave a brief answer about the timeline and turned to leave.

When did I become this person?

The old me wouldn't have asked that. The old me would have just said, "Okay, let me see how to push this forward," and then found a way to get it done. But now? Now my first instinct is to calculate the time cost. It’s like a math problem: work hours + time for Chen Hao + time owed to my parents = ? I can’t even solve it.

I talked to my parents again last night. Mom said her sleep has been terrible lately, stuck in the constant irritability of menopause. Dad’s blood pressure is up again. They didn't say it outright, but I could hear that "we are getting old" helplessness in their voices. As I listened, the guilt started sinking in—when was the last time I went home? Two months ago? When was the last time I called them first? I... I can't seem to remember.

And what am I so busy with over here? Busy proving that my promotion was earned. Busy keeping the spark alive with Chen Hao. Busy making sure my colleagues don't think I've lost my drive. Busy being a "reliable person."

But lately, this "reliability" feels like a facade. I stay reliable at work, but I’m becoming increasingly unreliable to my family.

I saw Li Xin’s face when she left. It wasn't reproach; it was more like disappointment. As if she were thinking, "Are you starting to run away, too?" I wanted to explain, to tell her I’m not running away, I’m just... just being pulled in too many directions.

But I didn't explain. I just said, "Let me think about it."

To be honest, I already have the answer. I know what I should do. I should have a heart-to-heart with Chen Hao and adjust our pace. I should tell Zhang Ming that I need to find a new balance between work and life. I should go home more often to be with my parents, or at least call them more than once a week.

But I’m still avoiding it. Because the moment I admit this, it means admitting "I can't be perfect." It means I have to lose something somewhere. And I am truly terrified of losing.

The weather outside is beautiful. But sitting here, I feel trapped.

I wonder if tomorrow will be any better.

# Conflict Resolution Framework—First Successful Application

**Date**: 2022-08-13  
**Time**: 14:00-15:30  
**Location**: Company Meeting Room  
**Tags**: #WorkshopApplication #Milestone #CareerDevelopment

---

## Event Description

This afternoon, while coordinating the allocation of user campaign resources between the Product and Operations departments, I applied the "Conflict Resolution Framework" taught by Mr. Li in the workshop.

This issue had been stuck for nearly a week. Product felt our campaign requests were too last-minute and lacked planning, while we felt they didn't understand the rhythm of user operations. Both sides had valid points, but neither would budge.

Today, I tried the method from the workshop:
1. First, I spoke with the heads of both departments individually to hear their core needs (not just their surface-level positions, but their actual pain points).
2. I discovered that Product’s core need was "predictability," while our core need was "flexibility."
3. During the meeting, instead of pointing fingers, I laid out both needs and asked, "Can we find a solution that ensures predictability while maintaining necessary flexibility?"
4. Ultimately, we brainstormed a compromise: regular campaigns are to be filed two weeks in advance, while sudden trending topics can follow an expedited process.

No one felt dismissed throughout the process; instead, everyone felt this solution was more reasonable than anything previously proposed.

Li Xin even made a point to tell me after the meeting, "You handled that really well today."

---

## Personal Reflection

I can hardly believe this framework actually works. When I first learned it in class, it felt a bit abstract, but applying it in real life felt completely different. It’s not that I suddenly became an expert; it’s just that I had a "new lens" through which to view the problem.

This makes me feel like... maybe these 8 weeks of investment weren't in vain?

I’m writing this down to remind myself: the next time I encounter a similar conflict, I can keep using this approach. I also want to see if I can become increasingly proficient with this framework.

---

**Status**: ✓ Completed  
**Mood**: A bit proud, but also a little embarrassed to admit how happy I am 😅

October 3, 2022, 10:15 PM

Exhausted. Truly exhausted.

It’s not physical fatigue; it’s that kind of mental drainage where your brain feels completely hollowed out. Today was the final session of the workshop, and Teacher Li said something that I’m still chewing over.

"Growth in competence doesn't happen overnight; it requires continuous learning and practice. Your current shortcomings are exactly where your future strengths lie."

I scribbled it down in my notebook and put a question mark next to it.

To be honest, I don’t really buy it. Or rather... I want to believe it, but I’m afraid to.

When I was working with Tableau this afternoon, looking at those complex data models and visualization interfaces, I just wanted to shut it all down. Seriously, my finger was already pressing the button to turn off the screen. My head was filled with voices saying, "I’ll never learn this," and "Why am I putting myself through this?" The moment the screen went black, I actually felt a sense of relief.

But ten minutes later, I turned the computer back on.

I don’t even know why. Maybe because Teacher Li’s words were still rattling around in my head, or maybe I just didn’t want to admit that I’d given up. I didn’t touch Tableau again; instead, I opened a basic Excel tutorial. Starting from the very beginning.

Halfway through the practice, I messed up. The formula was wrong, and the data was a total mess. I stopped for five seconds. Truly, five seconds. In those five seconds, I thought about deleting everything and starting over, about closing the file, about leaving it until tomorrow.

But I hit save.

Then I wrote a line in my notebook: Day 1: Failed, but persisted.

Looking at that sentence now, I kind of want to laugh. And kind of want to cry.

Wang Fang has been asking me what’s wrong lately, saying I haven’t been acting like myself. She’s right. I’m really not myself. The person who is always the first to suggest a group dinner, the one who can always liven up the atmosphere—that person is now spent all day at home, frowning at a computer screen.

But I can’t explain it. How do I tell her? Tell her I’ve realized I might not be that exceptional? Tell her I’ve started doubting if I’m actually cut out for moving up the career ladder? Tell her I’m currently in a constant tug-of-war with myself?

Saying these things out loud just feels so melodramatic.

But writing them here is different. Here, I can admit it—I’m scared. Scared that I won’t be able to learn it, scared that I’m actually just a "decent" executor and nothing more, scared of all the things I’ve been avoiding.

And scared that I might just give up like this.

Tomorrow, I continue. Excel Lesson 2.

I don’t know if Teacher Li is right, but at least... for now, I want to try.

Have you been thinking about this lately, too? I mean, about our pace, our choices, and all that. I feel like we’ve actually been thinking along the same lines all along; we just haven't really sat down to talk about it properly.

I’d love to hear what’s on your mind.

Chen Hao, do you have a minute?

I wanted to talk to you. It’s nothing major, just... I’ve been feeling a bit drained lately.

When I was video calling my parents last week, they mentioned some health issues. My dad’s blood pressure is a bit high, and my mom hasn't been sleeping well. I started looking things up and taking notes right away, trying to figure out how to help them improve. But the more I researched, the more anxious I got, because I realized how little I actually know about their day-to-day lives.

We video call once a week, and I’m always the one talking about work. I ask how they’re doing, but it feels like I’ve never truly made the time to be there for them. They’re both retired now, while I’m still here in a first-tier city, running around in circles.

It’s made me start rethinking how I spend my time. I put so much into work, and I really cherish the time I spend with you, but now I feel like I should be spending more time with them too. I feel like I’m being pulled in every direction, and I don’t know where the right compromise is.

Sometimes I wonder—did I actually lose something after getting that promotion?

I’m not complaining. It’s just... I’m feeling a bit lost. I wanted to hear your thoughts. What do you think about all this?

Fangfang, I’m still a bit at a loss for words to describe how I’m feeling right now.

Chen Hao got back from his business trip yesterday, and we were just sitting on the sofa chatting. I originally intended to tell him—as I usually do—that work’s been going well and life is fine, but... I just suddenly broke down. I told him everything: the pressure at work, my worries about our parents getting older, even how lonely I feel sometimes. I didn't try to act strong; I just let all my vulnerability out.

You know, I’ve never been like this in a relationship before. I always wanted to be the reliable one, the one who could handle everything. But yesterday, I just... let go.

Chen Hao didn’t try to placate me or give me some "chicken soup" motivational talk. He just listened, and then he offered some very practical ideas—like how we could plan a schedule together to visit our parents, or how we could brainstorm solutions when work gets stressful. He said, "We’ll face it together." It was that simple, but I felt truly seen.

I’m only just realizing now that real intimacy isn’t about two people being perfect; it’s about being able to completely let your guard down in front of each other. It’s not "I’m strong, so I can take care of you," but rather "We both have moments of weakness, but we can carry the weight together."

The feeling is honestly a bit overwhelming. I really want to talk to you about this. Have you ever had a moment like that in your relationship? I feel like our generation’s understanding of "intimacy" might be going through a real shift.

[Image: A black-and-white shot from a photography exhibition, a play of light and shadow capturing a raw, authentic moment]

Met an interesting soul today.

At the exhibition, he said that what a photographer captures is the subject’s most honest moment, stripped of all pretense. We drifted from one photo to the next, talking about art, life, and what it truly means to live meaningfully.

In that moment, I finally understood what it feels like to be truly seen.

It’s not about being rushed into the next stage of life; it’s about finally meeting someone who sees the world the same way you do.

It’s a good feeling.

Monday, October 16, 11:47 PM

Sitting on the bed now; Chen Hao is already asleep. I still want to write down what happened today, otherwise I feel like my head will just keep spinning.

To be honest, I’ve been in a really strange headspace since last week. The conflict between Wang Hao and Zhang Lin has kept me completely on edge. I even started thinking that maybe I’m just not cut out for management. It’s a terrifying thought—I’ve only been promoted for three months, and I’m already doubting myself.

But today, something changed.

I had one-on-one talks with both Wang Hao and Zhang Lin this afternoon. Truthfully, my initial plan was just to "fix the problem"—very much a "managerial" mindset. But when I sat down, I suddenly realized I didn't need to rush to judgment. I just... listened. I asked them why they felt that way, what difficulties they were facing at work, and where they thought the problem lay.

As I listened, it finally clicked. This wasn't a personality clash at all. It was just that the division of labor was too vague. They each thought the other was supposed to handle certain things, so in the end, nobody did. Then the blaming started. It was that simple.

I had this "Oh, so that’s it" moment. It felt like a weight that had been pressing on my chest was suddenly lifted.

I shared my observations with both of them, and then we mapped out the workflow moving forward—who is responsible for what, when to give feedback, and how to communicate. They both nodded in agreement. Zhang Lin even said, "Xiaoyu, this makes things so much clearer."

Walking out of the office, I realized that maybe my job isn't to "command" or "judge" like some authoritative figure, but to help them see the problem clearly. To build a set of rules that everyone understands.

Later, I even took the initiative to share this discovery with Li Xin. She said, "You handled that really well." Hearing those words made me breathe a huge sigh of relief.

Sitting here now, I feel... less afraid? It’s not that all the problems are solved, but at least I know what I can do. I don’t need to be that "high and mighty" kind of manager. I can do this job just by being myself—listening, understanding, and helping.

Maybe that’s what I bring to the table.

Chen Hao said I looked a bit more relaxed today. I didn't disagree.

Onward to tomorrow.

I finally got to apply the framework Mr. Li mentioned last time to this project. From the raw data to the visualization, and then to the underlying logic of user behavior—I’m still far from professional, but I can feel myself inching closer to becoming someone with a strategic mindset. It’s not a sudden "aha!" moment, but a very grounded, step-by-step sense of progress.

The challenge now is how to present these charts and findings to Zhang Ming so he understands why this perspective matters. I still need to figure out the best way to articulate that.

I’m exhausted, for real. My eyes are stinging and my shoulders are tight. But mixed in with that fatigue is a sense of fulfillment—the kind you get from doing something truly meaningful. It’s not just the satisfaction of checking off a task; it’s the feeling of "I learned something, and I actually did it."

Onward tomorrow. The next step is to try building a small user segmentation model using this same logic to see if I can find any patterns in the retention data. I’m a bit worried I might be overthinking things, but at least now I have the confidence to try.

Keep going.

Sunday Afternoon, June 25, 2023

I just turned down Wang Fang’s dinner invitation.

She said, "No worries, let's do it next time," and that sentence only made me feel worse. I know she understands, but it’s precisely that understanding that makes me feel like a deserter.

Sitting here in the office, looking at the dense markings on my calendar—project deadlines, dates with Chen Hao, scheduled video calls with my parents—I suddenly realized that I no longer have any "free" time. The things that used to bring me joy—pottery classes, dinners with friends, aimless window shopping—have all become "luxuries" that I have to "squeeze out time" for.

What’s even scarier is that I’ve stopped even trying to fight for that time.

Two weeks ago, Mom mentioned she wasn't sleeping well and Dad’s blood pressure was running high; it felt like someone hit the pause button on my life. Suddenly, the promotions at work and the little romantic moments with Chen Hao didn't seem so important anymore. But they *are* important. I can’t slack off at work—Zhang Ming has already seen my potential; I can’t neglect Chen Hao either—we haven't been together that long. And yet, I can’t just turn a blind eye to my parents.

I am being pulled apart.

Work needs my focus, Chen Hao needs my company, my parents need my care, Wang Fang needs me to be the "event organizer"... but there is only one of me.

The irony is that I used to enjoy socializing so much; I loved organizing events and seeing my friends happy. Now, I’m running away. Not because I don’t want to go, but because I simply don’t have the energy left. Every time I see an invitation from Wang Fang, my first reaction isn't "Great!" but "Do I even have the time?"

This isn't me. Or rather, it’s no longer me.

Where is the Lin Xiaoyu who could brighten up the office the moment she walked in? Where is the person who was always the first to suggest a Friday team outing? When did I start living so passively?

Maybe I was too greedy from the start. I thought I could achieve a perfect balance—the perfect employee, the perfect girlfriend, the perfect daughter, the perfect friend. Now I finally understand that this "perfection" was an illusion all along.

I have to make a choice. But I truly don't know what to choose.

I’ve been sitting in the office for nearly two hours now. I’m the only one here, and the lights feel a bit cold.

But that pivot sounded incredibly forced. Even I could hear it.

I wonder if Li Xin noticed. Does she think I’ve changed? That I’ve become less... open?

That’s the problem. I’ve always felt my greatest strength was being a good listener. Not the fake kind, but genuinely interested. In meetings, I was always the one asking questions first, building on other people’s ideas. That was my style. It made people want to work with me.

But now I’m a manager. I need to be decisive. I need to know what’s right and what’s wrong. I can’t just "let’s discuss it" everything.

So in that moment, I wanted to shut Li Xin’s idea down quickly. Because I felt like that’s what a manager is supposed to do.

Then I realized that wasn't me.

Then I changed my mind again.

So now, I’m neither the open version of myself nor a decisive manager. I’m just... awkward. That stiff thing stuck in the middle.

After the meeting, I sat in the office alone, replaying the scene over and over. "Did I react too fast?" "Does Li Xin think I’m being rigid now?"

I don’t know what to do.

I thought about messaging Zhang Ming to see what he thinks, but then I felt like it would make me look too insecure. I thought about calling Chen Hao, but he’s probably still busy. I thought about texting Wang Fang, but I’m afraid she’ll just try to comfort me, which would only make me more certain that something is actually wrong.

So I’m just sitting here.

I’m wondering if I’m just not cut out to be a manager. Maybe I should have just stayed the "reliable person"—executing tasks well and keeping everyone happy. Why did I have to climb the ladder?

But I know that’s not the right way to think. I can’t keep thinking like this.

I’m just... not ready. Or rather, I don’t know what "being ready" is even supposed to feel like.

Tomorrow goes on. I wonder if another moment like that will happen again.

# September 1, 2023 – Just past 11 PM

Just got out of the shower and I’m lying in bed, but my head is still buzzing.

He gave me five names. Li Xin, Wang Hao, Xiao Liu, Zhang Li, and the intern, Xiao Chen.

My fingers were shaking as I wrote those names down. They were actually shaking. I pretended to be tidying up some files just to try and calm myself down. Li Xin? Li Xin is my colleague right now. We’ve done so many event plans together; she knows my working style inside out. And now she’s supposed to call me "Lead"? It feels so strange.

But "management" is a different beast entirely. I have to make decisions. I have to handle conflicts. I’m responsible for the work of five people. If they mess up, it’s on me.

Chen Hao asked me what was wrong tonight. He said I kept checking my phone and seemed distracted during dinner. I just smiled and said, "Nothing, just a lot of work." I didn't tell him my fingers were shaking. I didn't tell him what I was actually thinking—can I be a good manager, a good girlfriend, and a good daughter all at once?

I’ve been thinking about how to spend more time with my parents lately. Dad had a bit of a cough during our last video call, and I’m worried. Mom said her back has been hurting, too. I was hoping I could make an extra trip home this month. But now? I have to lead a team. I have to attend more meetings. I have to...

Whatever, there’s no point thinking about this now.

It’s just... I’m the "reliable one," right? That’s what everyone says. What does reliable even mean? It means I can get things done. But right now, I really don’t feel like I can. I don’t even know what to say in my first meeting with the new team. Am I supposed to act authoritative? That’s not me. I’ve always been the person who listens and asks questions. Now, I have to be the one making the calls.

Wang Fang will definitely ask me tomorrow; she’s been bugging me for work updates. I know she’ll be thrilled for me. But I’m not ready to be "thrilled" yet. I’m still... I don’t know how to put it, I’m still processing.

My phone is still buzzing—probably someone liking a post. But I haven't even posted anything yet.

Forget it, I should sleep. I'll think about it tomorrow.

But... am I really ready for this?

11:15 PM.

Day four of Chen Hao’s business trip.

I’m sitting on the sofa in the living room. I haven’t even turned on the lights; I’m just sitting here in the dark. The glow from my phone screen illuminates my face as I look at the to-do list I made yesterday—the project proposal, household chores, things to organize before Chen Hao gets back, and my mom’s reminder to go home this weekend to check Dad’s physical exam report...

I suddenly realized that I might not be able to finish it all.

The thought makes me panic a little. It’s not the usual "oh, time is tight" kind of panic, but a deep sense of powerlessness. It’s like suddenly seeing my limits clearly and realizing I’m charging straight toward them.

I don’t know what’s wrong with me. Usually, I can handle this. Work, life, family—I’ve always been the one who can balance it all. My colleagues call me "reliable," my friends say I’m "full of energy," and I believed it too. But sitting here today, I just feel tired. So incredibly tired.

Tired to the point where I don’t even have the strength to wash up.

The apartment feels bigger when Chen Hao isn’t home. We’ve only been living together for three months, but I’ve already gotten used to him being here. When he’s around, even if we’re doing our own things, I don’t feel this kind of loneliness. Now he’s in Shenzhen and I’m here, separated by over a thousand kilometers and the reality that one person cannot do everything at once.

Mom said on our video call that Dad’s blood sugar is high again. I listened to her worries and tried to comfort her by saying "we’ll figure it out together," but the truth is, I don’t know what to do. What can I do? Can I fly back to be with them? Can I ensure nothing goes wrong with the project? Can I stop Chen Hao from traveling for work?

It feels like I’m letting a lot of people down at once.

Or rather, I’m letting myself down.

I always thought I had found that "sustainable rhythm." Life with Chen Hao, the sense of achievement at work, being there for my parents, my personal interests... I thought they could all coexist. But now it seems that when everything comes crashing down at once, I’m like a punctured balloon.

I didn't even reply to Wang Fang’s message. She asked me yesterday if I wanted to go to an exhibition this weekend. I didn't respond. I know she’ll understand, but I also know this isn't like me.

It’s 11:15 PM. Tomorrow, Chen Hao will message me to ask how my day was. I’ll say, "It was okay, just a bit tired." Then he’ll say, "Get some sleep early." And I’ll say, "Yeah, you too."

But I won’t tell him that I’m sitting in the dark, unable to see myself clearly.

Maybe I just need to sit like this for a while.

# Memo

**Date: October 12, 2023 – Evening**

## Key Insight – From Wang Fang
Conflicts are often not about the people, but about **processes and communication**. Hearing her say that made me feel a sudden sense of relief. It’s not necessarily that my management skills are lacking; it might truly just be that our division of labor hasn't been clearly defined.

## Action Plan for Tomorrow

**Step 1: Streamline the Workflow**
- Re-organize the current division of labor for community operations and user activities.
- Clarify who is responsible for which stage to avoid overlaps or omissions.
- Specifically look at where the boundaries lie between Li Xin’s responsibilities and those of the other colleagues.

**Step 2: Meeting Tomorrow Afternoon**
- Get the two of them together—not to "judge" who is right or wrong.
- Simply lay out the processes and responsibilities so everyone knows what the other is doing.
- Keep the tone calm; don't make it seem like I am "punishing" them.

**Step 3: Report to Zhang Ming**
- No need to use heavy terms like "team conflict."
- Just say, "Identified some workflow issues and am currently optimizing the division of labor."
- This makes it sound like I am proactively solving problems rather than reactively dealing with a crisis.

## Note to Self
Don’t overthink it. This isn't a big deal; it’s something every new manager goes through. Once the processes are clear and communication is in place, interpersonal issues usually resolve themselves. Do not take this as proof of "failure as a manager."

I’ll check later if anything else needs adjusting. For now, get some sleep. I need to be sharp tomorrow to get this done right.

# Thursday, August 11, 2022, Noon

Just another ordinary lunch break. I’m sitting in the corner of the office lounge, picking at a convenience store bento while a Tableau tutorial plays on my phone. Li Xin walked past and asked what I was watching. I just muttered, "Learning something new," and she walked off with a smile.

I feel a bit ridiculous.

During the first workshop session yesterday, Mr. Li shared a case study about an operations manager who drove user growth by 35% using data. Listening to it, I suddenly felt a pang of unease. I realized that for the past three years, every campaign I ran, every bit of user feedback I collected... it was all based on gut feeling. I’d say, "I feel like this is what users want," and then just execute. I never once used data to validate anything.

Zhang Ming once asked me why a certain campaign underperformed. My answer was just, "Maybe the timing was off," or "User response seemed a bit lukewarm." No numbers. No comparisons. Just... reliable execution followed by reliable excuses.

Thinking about it now makes me feel a bit suffocated.

When Chen Hao asked me how the workshop was last night, I told him how I felt. He didn't try to comfort me; he just said, "Well, now you know, so there's still time." I really wanted to snap back at him, but I couldn't find the words. Because he’s right. I do know now. But what comes after knowing? Can I actually change?

Lunch break is only an hour. I’ve spent 15 minutes on this Tableau tutorial, learning how to import data and create basic visualizations. It doesn't look hard. But then I think—this is just a tool. The real question is, do I have the kind of brain that "thinks in data"? Am I just naturally one of those emotional, intuition-reliant people?

I wonder if signing up for this workshop is just a form of escapism. An escape from the fact that being labeled as "reliable" is starting to stifle me. I tell myself I want to grow, to learn management, to level up. But in reality? Maybe I’m just running. Running from a comfortable spot toward something I’m not even sure about.

But if I don't run, I have to stay here forever. I’ll keep being called "reliable," keep making decisions based on feelings, and keep watching everyone around me take on more challenging things.

So, I’m still going to learn Tableau. I’m still going to push myself through these eight weeks.

I don’t know if this will actually change anything. But at least... at least I’m trying.

My bento is getting cold. There are 45 minutes left before I have to get back to work. I click on the next tutorial video.

[Article Title: Work-Life Balance Starts with Redefining "Success"]

---

I’ve been reflecting on a question lately: What does success actually mean?

I used to think success was all about promotions, hitting targets, and being recognized. But after handling some team issues recently, I’ve found myself feeling increasingly exhausted. It’s not physical fatigue, but mental—constantly wondering, "Am I doing well enough?" "Is this the right decision?" "Did I mess up somewhere?"

Then I realized, I might have had the definition of success backwards.

Success isn't about climbing the highest; it's about finding what matters most to you. For me, what is that? It’s being able to truly be there for my family, laughing with friends, and keeping a sense of warmth in my work, rather than being swallowed by power and perfectionism.

Lately, I’ve been thinking about what kind of leader I want to be. Not the cold, robotic type whose every decision must be flawless. But someone who can honestly say, "I’m not sure either, but let’s figure it out together." Someone who remembers that the person sitting across from them is a human being, not just a "team member."

This shift hasn't been as easy as I imagined. But I’m grateful to have people around who remind me—sometimes, being seen and understood is more important than being praised.

I’ll keep finding my way. It might not be perfect, but at least it’s real.

He asked so gently—"How do you feel about the timing?" It wasn't a nudge; it was just a question. And my first instinct wasn't "I'm not ready" or "Let's wait a bit." Instead, I started listing practical things: our current rent, our work rhythms, how I want to be there for my parents, whether our daily habits will clash...

I am thinking about this seriously. Not because of social expectations, not because of my age, but because I want to find the answer.

It really does feel different.

Chen Hao came in and sat down beside me. I didn't say anything right away, and neither did he. We just sat there together. This silence actually makes it easier to hear my own thoughts.

I’m in no rush to decide. But I’ve realized I am making a decision—using both my head and my heart. Financially we’re both okay, but the cost of living in a first-tier city is definitely a factor. Work-wise, I have more responsibilities now, and his projects are demanding. And then there are my parents; I want to make sure I have enough time and energy for them.

These aren't "obstacles." They are things we need to plan for together.

I think my entire perspective on this has shifted. It’s no longer a question of "if," but "how." It’s not about passively waiting for life to happen to me; it’s about actively designing it.

It feels grounding.

I’m a little tired, but I feel grounded.

Success isn't about climbing the highest; it's about finding what matters most to you and having the courage to live according to your own values.

This quote came to mind recently while I was handling some work matters. I used to think I had to find the perfect balance between career, life, and quality time—as if I couldn't miss a single beat or make a single mistake. But I’m starting to realize that life isn't a game of trying to keep several plates spinning at once.

Sometimes work takes up more time, sometimes being with family is the priority, and sometimes I just want to lounge at home with Chen Hao doing absolutely nothing. None of these are failures; they’re just different seasons of life.

When I shared this with my parents over video call, they told me, "You’ve grown up" 😂. When I talked to Chen Hao about it, he said, "That’s exactly why I love you."

Living imperfectly feels so much freer than pretending to live perfectly.

[Image: A photo of afternoon sunlight streaming through a window]

Wang Fang, I’m on the subway, just thinking about what you said.

You’re right—I really have changed. But it’s only now that I truly understand *how* I’ve changed.

It’s not that "I found the one" kind of excitement; you hit the nail on the head there. It’s more like... the feeling of being understood? It’s a bit hard to describe. Before, whenever I thought about my career, my head was always a mess—either overthinking everything or just shutting down entirely. Now, with his support, I can actually focus on these things more clearly. It’s not that he’s making decisions for me; it’s that he makes me feel like I don’t have to carry all these questions alone.

The most amazing thing is that we seem to have found our own rhythms. He knows I have to organize events on Fridays, so he just does his own thing. I know he has to revise requirement docs over the weekend, so I don’t cling to him. But it’s not coldness; it’s... a very comfortable kind of companionship?

To be honest, I was a little afraid that entering this stage would mean losing something—losing my freedom, or being tied down. But now I’ve realized that true intimacy isn’t about two people being glued together; it’s about both people standing firmly on their own and then *choosing* to be close. It sounds like a total cliché, but it’s what I’ve actually been feeling these past few months.

Remember when I asked you, "How do you know if he’s the right one?" My answer now isn't "because he’s perfect" or "because we’re so similar." It’s "because being with him allows me to see more clearly what I want for myself."

So when you say I’m different, I think that’s probably what you’re seeing—not some "lovestruck" high, but a sense of... stability from being truly seen?

I’m still thinking about whether to bring him along to your next outing. I used to think we should keep our social circles separate, but now I’m thinking, maybe I could try letting him in?

What do you think?

# April 10, 2022, 11:30 PM

I’ve been lying here for a long time, but sleep just won't come.

I was scrolling through Xiaohongshu in bed just now and came across a few posts about career pivots. They were all quite inspiring—moving from execution to management, how to build leadership, how to transition from a "doer" to a "leader." Watching them made me feel a bit restless, though I can't quite tell if that feeling is anticipation or panic.

I got up and grabbed a notebook to jot down a few keywords: core elements of management, cross-departmental coordination, team building... I stopped halfway through. Looking at myself in the mirror, I asked: Am I really cut out for management?

The person in the mirror looks so familiar. Energetic, passionate, reliable. These words have been used to describe me countless times at work. But looking at them now, they feel a bit like a box. I’ve been staying inside it quite comfortably—so comfortably that it feels like I’m... stagnating?

I don’t know when it started, but the word "reliable" has become like lukewarm water; it’s so comfortable that I never thought about jumping out. For two years now, I’ve handled event planning, user feedback, and cross-departmental communication with ease. My boss recognizes my work, and my colleagues trust me. But... that’s just it. That’s all there is.

I was chatting with Wang Fang recently, and she mentioned her department is hiring for a management role and asked if I’d be interested. I smiled and said, "Let me think about it," but in that moment, what flashed through my mind wasn't excitement. It was something strange—a mix of wanting to try and being terrified that I’m just not good enough. What am I afraid of? Afraid of finding out that this is all I’m capable of? Afraid of losing this sense of validation I have now?

I’ve looked at the brochure for Teacher Li’s Management Workshop several times. Every time I see it, I think, "This seems right for me," but I never actually sign up. It’s as if signing up would mean admitting that something is wrong with me right now, admitting that I need to change. It sounds contradictory; I can feel it myself.

Late nights are like this—all your defenses just crumble. During the day at the office, I’m still the one who proactively suggests Friday team-building activities, the one who listens to others with a smile. But lying here in bed, I’m asking myself: Is this what I want? Or am I just doing it because I’m good at it?

Work continues tomorrow. I’ll keep doing the things I’ve mastered. But it feels like something has already started to shift.

I don’t know what it is. But it’s there.

Chen Hao, did you see my post on WeChat Moments?

I felt a bit of regret right after posting it, thinking maybe I was oversharing. But I’ve actually received quite a lot of feedback. Liu Yang even left a comment saying, "This is the real Xiaoyu," which really touched me.

I realized I’ve been pretending I can handle everything, and honestly, it’s been exhausting. I’ve been processing what you told me the other day about "allowing yourself to be imperfect." Then, while chatting with Wang Fang today, she mentioned her own recent anxieties, and it hit me—it seems like everyone is just pretending to have it all under control.

After I posted that, a colleague messaged me privately saying she’s going through something similar and asked how I’ve been adjusting my mindset. It felt so good to actually help her; it felt much more meaningful than just sitting here overthinking things on my own.

I’ve been thinking about all of this while being home alone these past few days while you're away on your business trip. It’s been a little lonely, but things are also becoming a bit clearer. I’m not sure when you’ll be back, but I’d love to talk to you about some of these thoughts.

Actually, I’m most grateful for you. You didn't say things like "It’s okay, you’re doing great" that day. Instead, you asked me, "What do you need most right now?" It was that one question that gave me the courage to admit that I get tired, too.

😔
